| Sub-Module | Function | Marks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Device Management Module** | Handles device registration, de-registration, authentication, configuration, and OTA firmware updates. | 1 | | 2 | **Communication / Connectivity Module** | Performs protocol translation (MQTT ↔ HTTP, CoAP ↔ AMQP), message routing, and queue management. | 1 | | 3 | **Data Management Module** | Stores sensor data (hot + cold storage), performs filtering, aggregation, deduplication, and quality checks. | 1 | | 4 | **Service Management Module** | Supports service registration, discovery, lifecycle, and composition of multiple services into one. | 1 | | 5 | **Context Awareness & Semantic Module** | Adds semantic meaning to data using ontologies (e.g., SSN), performs event detection and reasoning. | 1 | | 6 | **Security & Privacy Module** | Provides authentication, authorization (RBAC/ABAC), encryption (TLS/DTLS), audit logging, anonymization. | 1 | | 7 | **Application Services / API Module** | Exposes RESTful APIs, WebSocket endpoints, and SDKs to the application layer. | 1 | > **Conclusion (1 Mark):** These seven sub-modules together form a robust middleware that enables **interoperability, scalability, and security** in any IoT deployment. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **(a)** What are the **design challenges** in building an IoT middleware? Explain any **five** in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Classify the **IoT Middleware architectures** into the three main paradigms with **one example platform** for each. Compare them on **scalability, real-time response, and resource demand**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Design Challenges in IoT Middleware (7 Marks):** The following are the **five primary design challenges** (any 5 to be explained): 1. **Heterogeneity (1.4 Marks):** IoT devices vary widely in hardware (Arduino, Raspberry Pi, ESP32), OS (Contiki, RIOT, Linux), and protocols (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP). Middleware must provide a **uniform interface** so the application does not have to handle this diversity. 2. **Scalability (1.4 Marks):** An IoT system may grow from 100 devices to millions. Middleware must scale **horizontally** (add more servers) and **vertically** (increase capacity) without architectural change. Cloud-native designs using Kubernetes, load balancers, and distributed queues are typically used. 3. **Real-Time Performance (1.4 Marks):** Applications like industrial control or autonomous vehicles require latency under **100 ms**. Middleware processing (security checks, context enrichment, storage) adds latency; it must be optimized using in-memory caches and edge processing. 4. **Security & Privacy (1.4 Marks):** IoT data is often sensitive (health, location, financial). Middleware must enforce **end-to-end encryption, device authentication, and fine-grained access control** while not becoming a performance bottleneck. 5. **Resource Constraints (1.4 Marks):** Some middleware must run on **edge devices** with limited CPU/RAM/battery. It must be lightweight — e.g., lightweight versions like **Eclipse Kura** for constrained gateways. --- **(b) Three Paradigms of IoT Middleware (7 Marks):** The KTU syllabus recognizes **three main middleware paradigms**: | # | Paradigm | Description | Example Platform | Marks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Application-Level / Service-Oriented** | Treats IoT resources as services (SOA); uses REST/SOAP. | **FIWARE** | 2 | | 2 | **Database-Oriented** | Provides a unified database abstraction; sensor data is queried like a DB. | **GSN (Global Sensor Networks)** | 2 | | 3 | **Agent-Based** | Autonomous agents run on devices; perform local decision-making. | **ActorNet** | 2 | **Comparative Table (1 Mark):** | Criterion | Application-Level | Database-Oriented | Agent-Based | |---|---|---|---| | **Scalability** | High | High | Limited | | **Real-Time Response** | Excellent | Moderate | Excellent | | **Resource Demand on Device** | Low | Low | High | | **Best Use Case** | Smart Cities, Smart Home | Environmental Monitoring | Robotics, Autonomous Systems | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. Students often **miss writing the conclusion sentence** in 14-mark answers. Always add a 1-2 line conclusion summarizing the answer. > 2. In the 5-Layer diagram, students **forget to label the layers clearly**. Always use **boxed text** or distinct blocks. > 3. When listing middleware sub-components, students **write vague terms like "processing module"** without explaining the *function*. KTU examiners award marks only for **function + example** pairs. > 4. Many students **confuse the Network Layer with the Middleware Layer**. Remember — the Network Layer only handles *transport*; the Middleware handles *abstraction, processing, and services*. > 5. For Part A questions, do **not write more than 1 page** — be concise and use bullet points. --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - ✅ Middleware is **Layer 3** of the 5-layer IoT Architecture — it sits between the Network and Application layers. - ✅ It solves **six core problems**: heterogeneity, scalability, real-time, security, discovery, and context-awareness. - ✅ The **seven sub-modules** of middleware are: Device Management, Communication, Data Management, Service Management, Context Awareness, Security, and Application Services. - ✅ Three architectural paradigms: **Application-Level (SOA), Database-Oriented, Agent-Based** — with examples FIWARE, GSN, and ActorNet respectively. - ✅ Middleware hides **protocol heterogeneity** (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, Zigbee) by converting them into a unified interface. - ✅ **Scalability** is a critical design constraint — middleware must handle exponential device growth. - ✅ The **latency equation** $L_{total} = L_{sense} + L_{transmit} + L_{process} + L_{middleware} + L_{app}$ is a frequent KTU formula. - ✅ Middleware should follow the design principles: **transparency, modularity, security-by-design, real-time performance, and resource efficiency**. - ✅ KTU frequently tests: definition (3 marks), diagram of 5-layer architecture (7 marks), sub-components (7 marks), and classification (7 marks). - ✅ **Pitfall to avoid**: Never confuse middleware with the Network Layer — middleware is *above* the network, providing *services*, not just packet transport. - ✅ Middleware enables **interoperability** — the most critical property of any IoT system.

topics.title: Service Oriented Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET634 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Service Oriented Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Service Oriented Architecture (SOA)** is a software design and architectural style in which business functionalities are packaged as **loosely coupled, interoperable services** that communicate with each other over a network through well-defined, platform-independent interfaces (typically using XML or JSON over standard protocols like HTTP).

In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, SOA provides the structural backbone that allows heterogeneous physical devices, sensors, cloud platforms, and applications to expose their capabilities as **discoverable, reusable, and composable services**. Instead of hardwiring device logic into monolithic applications, each "thing" advertises what it *can do* (a service), and other entities *consume* that capability on-demand.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **modern hotel**:

- You do not need to know *how* the kitchen cooks, *who* the electrician is, or *where* the laundry is located.
- You simply request services: "Room service, please," "Clean towels," "Wake-up call."
- Each service is delivered independently, you can mix and match them, and the hotel staff coordinates them behind the scenes.

SOA works the same way for software and IoT:
- A temperature sensor **publishes** a "Get Temperature" service.
- An air conditioner **discovers** it and **consumes** it.
- A mobile app **orchestrates** both without knowing either device's internal make or model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** SOA is one of the **four primary IoT reference architectures** recognized by KTU, alongside *Cloud-based, Domain-specific, and Communication-focused* architectures. It is the dominant model for **middleware-level IoT integration**.

### Core Characteristics of SOA (KTU Syllabus Mandate)

| # | Characteristic | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Loose Coupling** | Services have minimal knowledge of each other; changing one does not break the other. |
| 2 | **Service Reusability** | A service built once is consumed by many applications. |
| 3 | **Interoperability** | Works across platforms, languages, and devices. |
| 4 | **Discoverability** | Services can be found via a registry/directory. |
| 5 | **Composability** | Complex workflows built by chaining smaller services. |
| 6 | **Statelessness** | Each request is independent; no memory of prior calls. |
| 7 | **Autonomy** | Each service controls its own logic and resources. |

### Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Primary Service Description Standard:** **WSDL** (Web Services Description Language) — XML-based.
> - **Primary Message Exchange Standard:** **SOAP** (Simple Object Access Protocol) — over HTTP/SMTP.
> - **Service Registry Standard:** **UDDI** (Universal Description, Discovery, and Integration).
> - **Lightweight Alternative (often used in modern IoT):** **REST** with JSON payloads.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Operational Roles in SOA

SOA defines three distinct participants, often called the **"SOA Triangle"** or **"Publish-Find-Bind" model**:

1. **Service Provider** — Creates, hosts, and publishes the service. *(Example: A temperature sensor offering its readings.)*
2. **Service Registry / Broker** — Maintains a searchable catalog of available services. *(Example: UDDI registry, CoAP resource directory.)*
3. **Service Requester / Consumer** — Searches the registry, binds to a suitable service, and invokes it. *(Example: A mobile dashboard app.)*

### 2.2 The SOA Operational Flow (Step-by-Step Logic)

The lifecycle of any SOA interaction follows the **Publish–Find–Bind** pattern:

- **Step 1 — Publish:** The service provider registers its service description (interface, endpoint, semantics) in the registry.
- **Step 2 — Find:** The consumer queries the registry to discover services matching its functional needs and quality-of-service (QoS) criteria.
- **Step 3 — Bind:** The consumer establishes a runtime connection to the chosen service using the contract information retrieved.
- **Step 4 — Invoke:** Data and commands are exchanged according to the agreed protocol and message format.
- **Step 5 — Orchestrate (optional):** Multiple services may be chained to fulfil a composite business process.

### 2.3 Why SOA is Critical for IoT — The Engineering Rationale

| IoT Challenge | How SOA Solves It |
|---|---|
| Massive device heterogeneity (Zigbee, BLE, Wi-Fi, LoRa) | Service abstraction hides transport details. |
| Dynamic, ad-hoc device joining/leaving | Service discovery protocols (mDNS, CoAP RD) handle mobility. |
| Need to scale to billions of devices | Stateless services enable horizontal scaling. |
| Cross-domain data fusion (health + home + transport) | Composable services enable mash-ups. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Industrial IoT platforms like **Siemens MindSphere**, **AWS IoT Core**, and **Google Cloud IoT** all expose device data through SOA-style REST/Pub-Sub APIs, allowing ERP systems, analytics engines, and ML models to consume sensor data without direct device coupling.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although SOA is an architectural concept, KTU questions may test the **Service Coupling Metric** and **Composability Index** used to evaluate SOA quality.

| Parameter | Formula | Unit / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Coupling Metric (C)** | $C = \dfrac{N_{dc}}{N_{total}}$ | Ratio of inter-service dependencies to total service interactions. Lower is better. |
| **Reusability (R)** | $R = \dfrac{N_{reuse}}{N_{deploy}}$ | Average number of consumers per service. |
| **Service Granularity (G)** | $G = \dfrac{F_{per\_service}}{T_{system}}$ | Fraction of total business functions encapsulated per service. |
| **Discovery Latency (L)** | $L = t_{bind} - t_{query}$ | Time elapsed between query and successful binding (seconds). |
| **Composability Index (CI)** | $CI = \dfrac{N_{valid\_compositions}}{N_{theoretical}}$ | Probability of two services interoperating successfully. |
| **Statelessness Score (S)** | $S = 1 - \dfrac{N_{stateful\_calls}}{N_{total\_calls}}$ | Closer to **1** = fully stateless (ideal SOA). |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark Booster:** When asked to "justify why SOA is suitable for IoT," always mention **loose coupling, discoverability, and reusability** as the three pillars.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of SOA vs. Other IoT Architectures

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU comparison** frequently asked in Module 1.

| Feature | **SOA** | **Cloud-Based** | **Domain-Specific** | **Communication-Focused** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Service composition | Data storage & processing | Vertical industry needs | Network protocols |
| **Coupling** | Loose | Moderate | Tight | Tight |
| **Reusability** | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Protocol Stack** | SOAP / REST / CoAP | HTTP / MQTT | Proprietary | TCP / UDP / 6LoWPAN |
| **Device Heterogeneity Support** | Excellent | Good | Limited | Limited |
| **Discovery Mechanism** | Registry (UDDI / CoAP RD) | API Gateway | Hardcoded | None |
| **State Management** | Stateless preferred | Stateful | Stateful | Stateful |
| **Scalability** | High | Very High | Limited | Limited |
| **KTU Typical Use Case** | Cross-domain IoT mash-ups | Big-data IoT analytics | Smart grid, e-Health | WSN routing |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Service Coupling Metric for an IoT System

**Problem Setup (KTU Typical Numerical):**
A smart campus IoT deployment has **8 services** in its SOA registry. During one operational hour, **24 service-to-service interactions** were logged. Of these, **6 interactions** were direct cross-service dependencies (one service explicitly invoking another). Compute the **Coupling Metric (C)**.

**Given Data:**
- Total number of services: $N_{services} = 8$
- Total service interactions: $N_{total} = 24$
- Direct inter-service dependencies: $N_{dc} = 6$

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant formula from the cheat sheet.**

The Coupling Metric is defined as:

$$
C = \dfrac{N_{dc}}{N_{total}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

$$
C = \dfrac{6}{24}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the fraction.**

$$
C = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

A coupling value of **0.25** indicates that **25% of all service interactions involve direct cross-service calls**. Since SOA targets loose coupling, this is considered *acceptable*; values above **0.5** would be flagged as a tightly-coupled (anti-pattern) architecture.

**Step 5 — Engineering Recommendation.**

To reduce coupling further, the architect can:
- Introduce **asynchronous message brokers** (e.g., MQTT, RabbitMQ).
- Use **event-driven choreography** instead of direct service calls.
- Apply the **Service Façade Pattern** to decouple consumer logic.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Reusability Calculation

**Problem Setup:**
In the same smart campus, the temperature service is consumed by **3 different applications** (mobile app, weather dashboard, HVAC controller). The CO₂ monitoring service is consumed by **2 applications** (ventilation controller and analytics engine). If these are the only services in the system, calculate the **average Reusability (R)**.

**Given Data:**
- Service 1 (Temperature): $N_{reuse,1} = 3$ consumers.
- Service 2 (CO₂): $N_{reuse,2} = 2$ consumers.
- Both services are deployed: $N_{deploy} = 2$ total service instances.

**Step 1 — Apply the reusability formula for each service.**

For Service 1:

$$
R_1 = \dfrac{N_{reuse,1}}{N_{deploy,1}} = \dfrac{3}{1} = 3.0
$$

For Service 2:

$$
R_2 = \dfrac{N_{reuse,2}}{N_{deploy,2}} = \dfrac{2}{1} = 2.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average reusability of the system.**

$$
R_{avg} = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{2} = \dfrac{3.0 + 2.0}{2} = 2.5
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

An average reusability of **2.5** means each service is, on average, reused by 2.5 applications. SOA best practice targets **R ≥ 3** to justify the overhead of building services rather than monolithic modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SOA Reference Architecture Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following diagram illustrates the **SOA Triangle** with the addition of an IoT-specific device layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    devA[IoT Device A: Temperature Sensor]:::device
    devB[IoT Device B: Smart Light]:::device
    devC[IoT Device C: Door Lock]:::device
    gateway[Edge Gateway and Protocol Translator]:::edge
    registry[Service Registry and UDDI Catalog]:::registry
    broker[Message Broker: MQTT or AMQP]:::broker
    app1[Consumer App 1: Mobile Dashboard]:::consumer
    app2[Consumer App 2: Analytics Engine]:::consumer
    orch[Service Orchestrator and Workflow Engine]:::orchestrator

    devA -->|Publish Service| registry
    devB -->|Publish Service| registry
    devC -->|Publish Service| registry
    gateway -->|Aggregate Services| registry

    app1 -->|Find Service| registry
    app2 -->|Find Service| registry
    orch -->|Find Service| registry

    registry -->|Return WSDL or REST Contract| app1
    registry -->|Return WSDL or REST Contract| app2
    registry -->|Return WSDL or REST Contract| orch

    app1 -->|Bind and Invoke via SOAP or REST| broker
    app2 -->|Bind and Invoke via SOAP or REST| broker
    orch -->|Bind and Invoke via SOAP or REST| broker

    broker -->|Route Message| devA
    broker -->|Route Message| devB
    broker -->|Route Message| devC

    classDef device fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef edge fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef registry fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef broker fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef consumer fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef orchestrator fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Publish-Find-Bind Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Provider as Service Provider
    participant Registry as Service Registry
    participant Consumer as Service Consumer
    participant Device as Physical Thing

    Provider->>Registry: 1. Publish WSDL or REST contract
    Registry-->>Provider: 2. Acknowledgement and service ID
    Consumer->>Registry: 3. Query for temperature service
    Registry-->>Consumer: 4. Return endpoint and contract
    Consumer->>Device: 5. Bind to sensor endpoint
    Device-->>Consumer: 6. Return telemetry data
    Consumer->>Provider: 7. Invoke downstream service
    Provider-->>Consumer: 8. Return processed result
```

### 4.3 Layered SOA-IoT Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L5[Layer 5: Application and Business Process]:::top
    L4[Layer 4: Service Composition and Orchestration]:::orch
    L3[Layer 3: Service Registry and Discovery]:::reg
    L2[Layer 2: Service Communication Protocols SOAP REST MQTT CoAP]:::comm
    L1[Layer 1: Physical Things and Edge Devices]:::phy
    L0[Layer 0: Network and Transport TCP IP 6LoWPAN]:::net

    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5

    classDef top fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B88600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef orch fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef reg fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef comm fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef phy fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef net fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.4 Diagram Interpretation Notes

> [!NOTE]
> - **Solid arrows** in the Block Diagram represent the **Publish-Find-Bind** flow of contracts and metadata.
> - **Solid arrows** in the Sequence Diagram represent **runtime message exchanges** between consumer and provider.
> - The **Layered Stack** follows the **OSI-inspired convention** adopted by most KTU IoT textbooks, where each layer is logically independent and replaceable — a direct reflection of SOA's *loose coupling* principle.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Service Oriented Architecture (SOA) and list any three of its characteristics in the context of IoT.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Service Oriented Architecture (SOA) is a software design paradigm in which application functionality is decomposed into a set of **loosely coupled, interoperable services** that communicate through well-defined interfaces over a network. In IoT, it enables heterogeneous devices to expose their capabilities as discoverable and reusable services.

Three key characteristics (1 mark each):
1. **Loose Coupling** — Services have minimal dependency on each other.
2. **Reusability** — A service can be consumed by multiple applications.
3. **Discoverability** — Services can be located via a registry mechanism.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the roles of Service Provider, Service Registry, and Service Consumer in SOA.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Service Provider (1 mark):** Creates, hosts, and publishes a service with a clear contract (WSDL/REST API). *IoT example:* A temperature sensor publishing its readings.
2. **Service Registry (1 mark):** Acts as a centralized or distributed catalog where services are advertised and discovered using standards like UDDI or CoAP Resource Directory.
3. **Service Consumer (1 mark):** Searches the registry, retrieves the service contract, binds to the provider, and invokes the service at runtime. *IoT example:* A mobile dashboard displaying live sensor data.

---

### Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Module Internal Choice** pattern, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Service Oriented Architecture (SOA) reference model for IoT**. Explain the **Publish-Find-Bind** lifecycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **six key characteristics** of SOA and explain how each one addresses a specific challenge in IoT deployments. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) — SOA Reference Model and Lifecycle (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
The model comprises **three primary actors** connected by a **service registry**:

- **Service Provider** — Hosts the IoT device or gateway exposing functionality.
- **Service Registry** — Stores WSDL/REST descriptions; enables lookup.
- **Service Consumer** — Application, dashboard, or another service.

**Publish-Find-Bind Lifecycle (4 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Publish:** The provider registers the service contract in the registry.
2. **Find:** The consumer queries the registry for services matching functional and QoS requirements.
3. **Bind:** A runtime connection is established using the retrieved contract details.
4. **Invoke:** Data is exchanged using SOAP/REST/MQTT, and the service may participate in a **composite workflow** orchestrated by a higher-level engine.

---

##### Part (b) — Six Characteristics Mapped to IoT Challenges (7 marks)

| # | Characteristic | IoT Challenge Addressed | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Loose Coupling** | Device churn (joining/leaving) | Devices can be replaced without rewriting consumers. |
| 2 | **Reusability** | Cost of duplicating sensor logic | One temperature service feeds 10+ apps. |
| 3 | **Discoverability** | Dynamic environments | New devices auto-register and are found automatically. |
| 4 | **Interoperability** | Heterogeneous protocols (Zigbee, BLE) | Service contracts abstract away transport. |
| 5 | **Statelessness** | Scalability to millions of requests | Servers can be load-balanced without session memory. |
| 6 | **Composability** | Complex multi-step processes | A "smart home" workflow chains sensor + actuator services. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **draw the SOA diagram without naming the three roles explicitly**. Always label *Provider, Registry, Consumer* on the diagram — a missing label costs 1 mark. Also, do **not** confuse **UDDI** (a registry standard) with **WSDL** (an interface description language) — this is a common 2-mark deduction.

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2023]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **SOA, Cloud-based, and Domain-specific IoT architectures** under the heads: focus, coupling, reusability, scalability, and typical use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart factory deploys **5 IoT services** with the following consumer counts: Service A = 4, Service B = 2, Service C = 6, Service D = 1, Service E = 3. Compute the **average Reusability (R)** of the system and state whether the deployment meets the SOA best-practice threshold. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Head | SOA | Cloud-Based | Domain-Specific |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** (1) | Service composition | Storage & analytics | Vertical industry logic |
| **Coupling** (1.5) | Loose | Moderate | Tight |
| **Reusability** (1.5) | High | Medium | Low |
| **Scalability** (1.5) | High | Very High | Limited |
| **Typical Use Case** (1.5) | Cross-domain mash-ups (e.g., smart city) | Big-data IoT platforms (AWS IoT) | Smart grid protocols (IEC 61850) |

---

##### Part (b) — Reusability Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the formula (1 mark).**

$$
R_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum R_i}{N_{services}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute per-service reusability (3 marks — 0.5 each + 0.5 for setup).**

Each service is deployed once, so $N_{deploy,i} = 1$.

$$
R_A = 4,\quad R_B = 2,\quad R_C = 6,\quad R_D = 1,\quad R_E = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Sum and average (2 marks).**

$$
\sum R_i = 4 + 2 + 6 + 1 + 3 = 16
$$

$$
R_{avg} = \dfrac{16}{5} = 3.2
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation and decision (1 mark).**

Since $R_{avg} = 3.2 \geq 3$, the deployment **meets the SOA best-practice reusability threshold** of 3 consumers per service on average. Service D (only 1 consumer) is a candidate for *decommissioning* or *merging* with another service to improve overall efficiency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to **show the substitution step** clearly in the formula. Always write $R = \dfrac{N_{reuse}}{N_{deploy}}$ explicitly before plugging in values. Also, ensure the **final unitless result** is followed by a one-line **engineering interpretation** — examiners award 1 mark specifically for the interpretive comment.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**
>
> - **SOA =** "Loose-coupling + Discoverability + Reusability" — memorize this phrase verbatim.
> - **Three roles:** Provider, Registry, Consumer (UDDI is the registry, WSDL is the description, SOAP is the protocol).
> - **Lifecycle:** Publish → Find → Bind → Invoke.
> - **Modern IoT variant:** REST + JSON + MQTT + CoAP Resource Directory replaces heavyweight SOAP/UDDI in constrained environments.
> - **Coupling Metric formula:** $C = \dfrac{N_{dc}}{N_{total}}$ — always shows up in 7-mark numericals.
> - **Reusability formula:** $R = \dfrac{N_{reuse}}{N_{deploy}}$ — best-practice threshold is **R ≥ 3**.
> - **Statelessness Score:** $S = 1 - \dfrac{N_{stateful\_calls}}{N_{total\_calls}}$; ideal value is **1**.
> - **SOA is preferred for cross-domain IoT** (smart city, integrated healthcare + transport) — NOT for high-frequency WSN telemetry.
> - **Comparison table** with Cloud, Domain-specific, and Communication architectures is the **most frequently repeated 7-mark question** in Module 1.
> - **Always label the three SOA actors in diagrams** — missing labels = guaranteed 1-mark cut.
> - **Avoid using "SOA" and "Web Services" interchangeably** — SOA is the *architecture*; web services are one *implementation* of it.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: M2M Communication and IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET634 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** M2M Communication and IoT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# M2M Communication and IoT — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine-to-Machine (M2M) Communication** is an autonomous, direct data-exchange paradigm between physical computing devices (sensors, actuators, embedded systems, smart meters, vehicles, RFID tags) over wired or wireless communication channels, **without requiring human intervention or manual interpretation** in the data transaction loop.

**Internet of Things (IoT)**, in contrast, is the global, Internet-protocol-based ecosystem that interconnects uniquely identifiable "Things" (devices embedded with sensors, actuators, processors, and transceivers) using the public Internet as the communication backbone, enabling **pervasive sensing, intelligent analytics, and actuation** at a global scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition Snippet (Write verbatim in exams):**
> *"M2M refers to the autonomous communication between machines/devices using any communication channel (wired or wireless). IoT is an evolution of M2M that integrates Internet protocols, cloud computing, and big-data analytics to create a globally connected, intelligent network of physical objects."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine two vending machines in a faraway warehouse:

- **M2M Analogy**: One vending machine notices its stock of Diet Coke dropped below 10 cans. It **automatically pings another vending machine** across the warehouse via a private radio link, requesting a refill transfer. **No person, no Internet, no smartphone** is involved — just two machines talking machine-language over a closed channel.

- **IoT Analogy**: Now imagine the same vending machine stock data is uploaded to a **cloud dashboard** over Wi-Fi/4G. The store manager sees the alert on a mobile app, an AI model predicts future stock-out timing based on weather and event data, and an autonomous drone is dispatched. Here, **the Internet, cloud analytics, and humans-in-the-loop (passively)** are integrated.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic for Exams:**
> - **M2M = Machine ↔ Machine** (Local, point-to-point, often private)
> - **IoT = Machine ↔ Cloud ↔ People ↔ Process** (Global, IP-based, open)

## 1.3 Core Distinction Matrix — M2M vs IoT vs IoE

| Parameter | **M2M** | **IoT** | **IoE (Internet of Everything)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Communication Type** | Point-to-Point (Device ↔ Device) | Many-to-Many (Device ↔ Cloud ↔ Device) | People + Process + Data + Things |
| **Connectivity Backbone** | Private cellular / RF / Serial / CAN / Modbus | Internet (IP-based, TCP/UDP), Wi-Fi, LTE-M, NB-IoT, LoRa | Internet + Cloud + Edge + Fog |
| **Data Destination** | Local server / SCADA | Cloud / Fog / Edge Analytics | Cloud + AI/ML Pipelines |
| **Intelligence Location** | At the device / local controller | Distributed (Device + Cloud) | Distributed (Device + Edge + Cloud) |
| **Human Involvement** | Zero (fully autonomous) | Minimal / Indirect | High (Decision support) |
| **Data Volume** | Low – Moderate | High – Massive (Big Data) | Massive + Behavioral |
| **Standards Body** | ETSI M2M, 3GPP, OMA | IETF, IEEE, W3C, OneM2M | Cisco-led, IEEE, Industrial |
| **Typical Application** | SCADA, Telemetry, Smart Metering | Smart Home, Wearables, Connected Cars | Smart Cities, Digital Twins |
| **Protocol Examples** | Modbus, CAN bus, ZigBee Pro | MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/REST, AMQP, LwM2M | All + WebSocket, gRPC, OPC-UA |
| **Scale** | Hundreds to Thousands | Thousands to Billions | Trillions of interactions |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Bandwidth of a typical NB-IoT channel:** **180 kHz**
> - **LoRaWAN Spreading Factor (SF) range:** **SF7 to SF12**
> - **Standard MQTT QoS levels:** **0 (At most once), 1 (At least once), 2 (Exactly once)**
> - **Maximum CoAP Message Size:** **1152 bytes** (to avoid IP fragmentation)
> - **OneM2M Reference Architecture:** **ETSI TS 118 101 / TS 118 102**
> - **Default MQTT port:** **1883** (plain) / **8883** (TLS encrypted)
> - **Default CoAP port:** **5683**
> - **CoAP over DTLS port:** **5684**
> - **3GPP M2M Standardization Release:** **Release 10 onwards (Machine-Type Communications - MTC)**

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 M2M Communication — Operational Architecture Breakdown

M2M systems, as per **ETSI TS 102 690**, consist of **two principal domains**:

### **A. M2M Device Domain (Sensors + Actuators + Communication Module)**

- **Sensors**: Convert physical phenomena (temperature, pressure, motion, light) into electrical signals.
- **Actuators**: Convert electrical commands into physical actions (valve open, motor rotate, LED blink).
- **Communication Module**: Cellular (LTE-M, NB-IoT), Short-range (ZigBee, BLE), or wired (RS-485, CAN).
- **M2M Area Network (MAN)**: Local connectivity among devices (e.g., ZigBee mesh, BLE star).

### **B. M2M Application/Network Domain (Server + Middleware + Apps)**

- **M2M Gateway / Concentrator**: Aggregates data from many devices, performs protocol translation.
- **M2M Server**: Receives, stores, and processes telemetry data.
- **Middleware / Application Platform**: Handles device management, billing, security, and APIs.
- **Applications**: User dashboards, SCADA HMI, ERP integration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Must-Know Sub-Layers of the M2M Communication Stack:**
> 1. **Sensing / Actuation Layer** (Physical transducers)
> 2. **Connectivity / Access Layer** (PHY + MAC — LTE, Wi-Fi, ZigBee)
> 3. **Network / Transport Layer** (TCP/UDP, IPv4/IPv6, 6LoWPAN)
> 4. **Middleware / Service Layer** (OneM2M, LWM2M, OPC-UA)
> 5. **Application Layer** (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, XMPP, AMQP)

## 2.2 IoT Communication Architecture (Layered View)

IoT architecture is conventionally expressed in **5 layers**:

1. **Perception Layer** — Sensors, RFID, GPS, Smart devices capturing environmental data.
2. **Network Layer** — 3G/4G/5G, Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN, Satellite, Routing + Gateways.
3. **Middleware / Transport Layer** — Encapsulation, Protocol conversion (MQTT broker, CoAP server).
4. **Application Layer** — Smart Home, Smart Health, Industrial Automation, Smart Agriculture.
5. **Business Layer** — Analytics, AI/ML, Decision Support, Revenue Models, SLAs.

## 2.3 M2M Value Chain — The Four Pillars

The **M2M Value Chain** (mandatory KTU diagram concept) consists of:

1. **Equipment Vendors** — Hardware manufacturers (chipset + module makers).
2. **Connectivity Providers** — Cellular operators, LPWAN network operators.
3. **Solution Providers / System Integrators** — Combine hardware + software + connectivity.
4. **Application Service Providers (ASPs)** — End-user applications and analytics.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" behind Value Chain:** Each tier adds value. For example, a sensor vendor sells the chip → an M2M module integrator packages it with a modem → a connectivity provider delivers network coverage → a solution provider builds dashboards → the customer pays a subscription (recurring revenue model).

## 2.4 Communication Protocols — Deep Comparison

### **A. MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)**

- **Architecture**: Publish/Subscribe over TCP (broker-centric).
- **Designed by**: IBM (1999) / OASIS Standard (2014).
- **Key Feature**: Topic-based routing, lightweight header (~2 bytes), ideal for constrained devices.
- **QoS Levels**: 0, 1, 2 (see Section 1.4).
- **Use Cases**: Telemetry, push notifications, mobile-to-cloud messaging.

### **B. CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)**

- **Architecture**: Request/Response (RESTful) over UDP.
- **Standardized by**: IETF RFC 7252.
- **Key Feature**: Maps 1-to-1 with HTTP semantics (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) but optimized for low bandwidth.
- **Reliability**: Confirmable (CON) vs Non-confirmable (NON) messages.
- **Use Cases**: Smart Energy, Home Automation, constrained sensor networks.

### **C. AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol)**

- **Architecture**: Broker-oriented with Queues, Exchanges, Bindings.
- **Standardized by**: OASIS / ISO/IEC 19464.
- **Key Feature**: Enterprise-grade reliability, transaction support, pluggable security.
- **Use Cases**: Banking, Industrial IoT (IIoT), Enterprise middleware.

### **D. HTTP/REST**

- **Architecture**: Client-Server over TCP.
- **Drawback**: Heavy headers (~1KB+), TCP handshake overhead, not suitable for constrained nodes.
- **Use Case**: Cloud-facing APIs, Web dashboards, non-constrained devices.

### **E. DDS (Data Distribution Service)**

- **Architecture**: Publish/Subscribe with no broker (decentralized).
- **Standardized by**: OMG (Object Management Group).
- **Use Case**: Real-time, mission-critical systems (avionics, autonomous vehicles, defense).

### **F. LwM2M (Lightweight M2M)**

- **Architecture**: CoAP-based device management protocol.
- **Standardized by**: OMA SpecWorks.
- **Use Case**: Device management (firmware update, remote configuration) for cellular IoT devices.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All exam-relevant formulas for M2M/IoT quantitative problems are tabulated below. Memorize these.**

| **Formula / Concept** | **Equation** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity** | $C = B \cdot \log_2 \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $C$ = Capacity, $B$ = Bandwidth, $S/N$ = Signal-to-Noise Ratio | **bits/second (bps)** |
| **Spectral Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{B}$ | $R_b$ = Bit rate, $B$ = Bandwidth | **bits/s/Hz** |
| **SNR in dB** | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | S = Signal power, N = Noise power | **dB** |
| **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ = Distance (km), $f$ = Frequency (MHz) | **dB** |
| **Received Power (Friis Eq.)** | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | $P_t$ = Tx power, $G_t, G_r$ = Antenna gains, $\lambda$ = wavelength | **Watts (W)** |
| **Bit Error Rate (BPSK AWGN)** | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2\,\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | $E_b$ = Energy/bit, $N_0$ = Noise spectral density | **dimensionless** |
| **LoRaWAN Time-on-Air** | $T_{\text{air}} = T_{\text{sym}} \cdot \left(N_{\text{preamble}} + \text{PL}_{\text{syms}}\right)$ | $T_{\text{sym}} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SF}}}{B}$ | **seconds (s)** |
| **Energy per Bit** | $E_b = \dfrac{P_t}{R_b}$ | $P_t$ = Transmit power, $R_b$ = Bit rate | **Joules/bit** |
| **Network Throughput** | $\text{Thr} = \dfrac{\text{Total Payload Bits}}{\text{Total Transmission Time}}$ | — | **bps** |
| **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** | $\text{PDR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{received}}}{N_{\text{transmitted}}}$ | — | **dimensionless (0–1)** |
| **End-to-End Delay** | $D_{e2e} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{proc}}$ | All in seconds | **seconds (s)** |
| **Jitter** | $\sigma_D = \sqrt{\mathbb{E}\!\left[(D - \mu_D)^2\right]}$ | Standard deviation of delays | **seconds (s)** |
| **M2M Device Density (per cell)** | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{\text{devices}}}{A_{\text{cell}}}$ | Number of devices per unit area | **devices/km²** |
| **Net Present Value (M2M Business)** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{R_t - C_t}{(1 + r)^t} - C_0$ | $R_t$ = Revenue, $C_t$ = Cost, $r$ = Discount rate | **Currency** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grid (M2M)**: Each smart meter reports hourly consumption to a central concentrator via PLC/RF — no human meter reader.
- **Connected Vehicles (IoT)**: A car's ECU publishes telemetry to the cloud using MQTT — fleet managers get real-time location and diagnostics.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: A factory floor with 10,000 OPC-UA enabled machines streams vibration data using DDS to a predictive-maintenance AI engine, preventing costly downtime.
- **Precision Agriculture (M2M + IoT)**: Soil-moisture sensors (ZigBee M2M) report to a gateway, which forwards to a cloud IoT platform, which triggers irrigation via MQTT.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Shannon-Hartley Capacity (Most Asked in KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A noisy M2M channel has a bandwidth of **B = 200 kHz** and an SNR of **20 dB**. Determine the maximum achievable data rate for an M2M telemetry link.

### Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

Substituting $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20$:

$$
20 = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) = 2
$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10^{2} = 100
$$

> So the linear SNR = **100**.

### Step 2 — Apply the Shannon-Hartley Theorem

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2 \left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

Substituting $B = 200{,}000$ Hz and $\frac{S}{N} = 100$:

$$
C = 200{,}000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 100)
$$

$$
C = 200{,}000 \cdot \log_2(101)
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Logarithm

$$
\log_2(101) = \frac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.00432}{0.30103} \approx 6.6582 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Result

$$
C = 200{,}000 \times 6.6582 \approx 1{,}331{,}640 \text{ bps}
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 1.33 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is the **theoretical maximum**. Real systems achieve 50–70% of this due to coding overhead, channel impairments, and protocol overhead.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of LoRaWAN Time-on-Air (End-Device Telemetry)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** An M2M soil sensor transmits a 20-byte payload over LoRaWAN with **Spreading Factor SF = 9**, **Bandwidth B = 125 kHz**, **Coding Rate CR = 1**, and **Header mode enabled (n = 0)**.

### Step 1 — Symbol Duration Formula

$$
T_{\text{sym}} = \frac{2^{\text{SF}}}{B}
$$

Substituting SF = 9, B = 125,000 Hz:

$$
T_{\text{sym}} = \frac{2^9}{125{,}000} = \frac{512}{125{,}000}
$$

$$
T_{\text{sym}} = 4.096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4.096 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 2 — Preamble Symbols

$$
N_{\text{preamble}} = (n + 4.25) \text{ symbols}
$$

With $n = 0$:

$$
N_{\text{preamble}} = (0 + 4.25) = 4.25 \text{ symbols}
$$

### Step 3 — Payload Symbol Count (LoRaWAN Formula)

$$
n_{\text{payload}} = 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil\frac{8 \cdot \text{PL} - 4 \cdot \text{SF} + 28 + 16 - 20 \cdot H}{4 \cdot (\text{SF} - 2\text{DE})}\right\rceil \cdot (\text{CR} + 4), 0\right)
$$

Where PL = 20 bytes (payload), H = 1 (header enabled), DE = 0 (low-data-rate optimization off), CR = 1.

**Numerator:**
$$
8 \cdot 20 - 4 \cdot 9 + 28 + 16 - 20 \cdot 1 = 160 - 36 + 28 + 16 - 20 = 148
$$

**Denominator:**
$$
4 \cdot (9 - 0) = 36
$$

**Ratio (raw):**
$$
\frac{148}{36} = 4.1111
$$

**Ceiling & CR scaling:**
$$
\left\lceil 4.1111 \right\rceil = 5, \quad \text{then} \quad 5 \cdot (1 + 4) = 25 \text{ symbols}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Time-on-Air

$$
T_{\text{air}} = T_{\text{sym}} \cdot \left(N_{\text{preamble}} + n_{\text{payload}}\right)
$$

$$
T_{\text{air}} = 4.096 \text{ ms} \times (4.25 + 25)
$$

$$
T_{\text{air}} = 4.096 \text{ ms} \times 29.25
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{air}} \approx 119.81 \text{ ms} \approx 0.12 \text{ s}}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** LoRaWAN's chirp-spread spectrum trades data rate for range. Higher SF = longer time-on-air = higher energy consumption. For battery-powered M2M nodes, SF must be carefully tuned.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — M2M vs IoT Communication Stack Mapping

| **OSI Layer** | **M2M Implementation** | **IoT Implementation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Application (L7)** | SCADA Modbus RTU, M-Bus, DNP3 | MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/REST, AMQP, LwM2M, OPC-UA |
| **Transport (L4)** | TCP (rare), mostly proprietary | TCP / UDP / DTLS / TLS |
| **Network (L3)** | IPv4 (NATed), rarely IPv6 | IPv6 + 6LoWPAN / RPL |
| **Data Link (L2)** | PLC, ZigBee Pro, RS-485, CAN, NFC | 802.15.4, BLE 5.0, LTE-M, NB-IoT, Wi-Fi 6 |
| **Physical (L1)** | RS-232, RS-485, 868/915 MHz RF, PLC | Sub-GHz RF, 2.4 GHz, 5G FR1, mmWave (V2X) |
| **Identification** | IMEI, ICCID, MSISDN, MAC | URI (CoRE Link Format), UUID, EPC (RFID) |
| **Security** | SIM-based (cellular), proprietary | DTLS, TLS 1.3, X.509, OSCORE, Blockchain |

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> Each question below carries **3 marks**. Write crisp 3–5 sentence answers with a diagram where applicable.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"Differentiate between M2M and IoT communication with suitable examples."** *(3 marks)*

#### Model Answer:

| **Aspect** | **M2M** | **IoT** |
|---|---|---|
| **Communication Scope** | Device-to-Device, Local/Private | Device-Cloud-Device, Global/Internet |
| **Protocols** | Modbus, CAN, ZigBee Pro, DNP3 | MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP, LwM2M |
| **Intelligence** | Embedded, Local | Distributed (Device + Edge + Cloud) |
| **Human Intervention** | None | Indirect (via apps/dashboards) |
| **Example** | Industrial PLCs talking via CAN bus in a factory | A wearable fitness band uploading heart-rate to a cloud dashboard via MQTT over Wi-Fi |

> **1-mark** for definition + **1-mark** for any 3 differences + **1-mark** for examples.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**
**"List and briefly explain any THREE M2M communication protocols."** *(3 marks)*

#### Model Answer:

1. **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** — A lightweight **publish/subscribe** protocol over TCP, designed for constrained devices and unreliable networks. Uses a central broker (e.g., Mosquitto). Has 3 QoS levels: 0, 1, 2. *(1 mark)*

2. **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** — An **HTTP-like RESTful** protocol over UDP (RFC 7252). Optimized for low-power, lossy networks. Supports confirmable/non-confirmable messages and observe pattern. *(1 mark)*

3. **AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol)** — An enterprise-grade **broker-based protocol** with queuing, routing, and transaction support. Widely used in banking, industrial IoT, and large-scale message brokers (e.g., RabbitMQ). *(1 mark)*

> **Tip:** Always pair each protocol with its **layer, transport, and key feature**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Full-Answer Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Module has Part-B questions of 14 marks with internal choice (a or b). Below is one full question with two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each, plus an alternative **Question B** choice.

---

### **Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**Question A (14 marks):**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **ETSI M2M Reference Architecture** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of **M2M Device Domain**, **M2M Network Domain**, and **M2M Application Domain**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the **5-layer IoT Architecture**. Discuss the role of each layer in enabling smart-city applications. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) — 7 marks**

- **Introduction** (1 mark): M2M architecture standardized by **ETSI TS 102 690** (later evolved to **OneM2M TS 118 101**). It enables end-to-end communication between devices without human intervention.

- **M2M Device Domain** (2 marks): Contains the **M2M Devices** (sensors, actuators, communication modules) and the **M2M Area Network (MAN)** — ZigBee, BLE, M-Bus, PLC. Devices collect data and forward to the gateway.

- **M2M Network Domain** (2 marks): Includes the **Access Network** (cellular, Wi-Fi, satellite), **Core Network** (3GPP backbone), **M2M Service Platform** (device management, data collection, security), and **Middleware / Common Services**.

- **M2M Application Domain** (2 marks): Houses **M2M Applications** (SCADA, fleet management, smart metering), **BSS/OSS** (billing, operations), and **Network Management Functions**.

> **Examiner Tip:** Draw the block diagram first (carries 2 marks by itself), then label each domain. Always mention **TS 102 690** or **OneM2M** — this gives 0.5 extra credit.

---

**(b) — 7 marks**

- **Perception Layer** (1.5 marks): Physical sensors (temperature, humidity, motion, RFID) + actuators. Captures raw analog/digital data from the environment.

- **Network Layer** (1.5 marks): 5G/4G/LTE-M, NB-IoT, Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN, satellite. Routes data from gateways to cloud servers using IP-based protocols (TCP/UDP/IPv6/6LoWPAN).

- **Middleware / Transport Layer** (1.5 marks): Protocol translation (MQTT brokers, CoAP servers), data aggregation, security, and device management services.

- **Application Layer** (1.5 marks): Domain-specific apps — smart traffic, smart lighting, waste management, smart parking, citizen dashboards.

- **Business Layer** (1 mark): AI/ML analytics, decision-making, SLAs, revenue models, regulatory compliance. Converts raw data into actionable intelligence.

> **Examiner Tip:** A neat labeled diagram with all 5 layers wins you 2 marks instantly. Map **each layer** to a smart-city use case (e.g., perception = traffic camera; application = traffic light controller).

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **MQTT, CoAP, and AMQP** protocols. Tabulate at least 8 differentiating parameters and state one best-fit application for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is the **M2M Value Chain**? Explain its four pillars with a diagram. Why is the **OneM2M** standardization important for global M2M/IoT interoperability? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) — 7 marks** — See comparison table:

| **Parameter** | **MQTT** | **CoAP** | **AMQP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP | UDP | TCP |
| **Architecture** | Pub/Sub | Req/Res | Pub/Sub + Queues |
| **Header Size** | 2 bytes fixed | 4 bytes base | 8+ bytes |
| **QoS** | 3 levels (0,1,2) | CON/NON + token | 2 levels (settle/unsync) |
| **Security** | TLS | DTLS | TLS + SASL |
| **Port** | 1883 / 8883 | 5683 / 5684 | 5672 / 5671 |
| **Broker Required** | Yes | No (server optional) | Yes |
| **Best-Fit App** | Cloud Telemetry | Smart Energy / Home | Enterprise / Banking |

Each row = ~0.5 mark. Applications = 2 marks (MQTT telemetry, CoAP smart energy, AMQP banking). *(7 marks)*

---

**(b) — 7 marks**

- **Definition** (1 mark): The M2M Value Chain is the end-to-end ecosystem of stakeholders enabling M2M service delivery.

- **Four Pillars** (3 marks):
  1. **Equipment Vendors** — Chipset & module makers (e.g., Qualcomm, Quectel).
  2. **Connectivity Providers** — Cellular + LPWAN operators.
  3. **Solution Providers** — System integrators + middleware platforms.
  4. **Application Service Providers (ASPs)** — End-user applications.

- **Diagram** (1 mark): Flow chain with revenue flow back.

- **OneM2M Importance** (2 marks): **OneM2M** (founded 2012, ETSI/TTA/CCSA/TTA/TSDSI collaboration) provides a **global, horizontal, common service layer** that abstracts device heterogeneity, ensures **interoperability** across vendors, defines standardized APIs (RESTful HTTP/CoAP/MQTT bindings), and reduces fragmentation. Without it, vertical silos would dominate.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in M2M/IoT Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing M2M with IoT** — Many students write them as synonyms. They are **NOT** the same; M2M is a subset/precursor of IoT. Always state the distinction.
> 2. **Skipping the Block Diagram** — In 14-mark questions, an unlabeled or missing diagram forfeits **2–3 marks** easily. Always draw and label.
> 3. **Forgetting the Standard Bodies** — KTU values naming **ETSI TS 102 690**, **OneM2M**, **IETF RFC 7252**, **OASIS MQTT v5.0**, **3GPP Release 10+**.
> 4. **Ignoring QoS Levels of MQTT** — When discussing MQTT, you MUST mention QoS 0/1/2. Forgetting = 1 mark lost.
> 5. **M2M Value Chain Incomplete** — Always include all 4 pillars + revenue flow direction. Half the chain = 0 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Master these 20 high-frequency KTU points before the exam:**

- [x] **M2M = autonomous device-to-device communication; IoT = IP-based, internet-integrated evolution of M2M.**
- [x] **ETSI M2M Architecture** has 2 domains: Device + Network (+ Application).
- [x] **IoT Architecture** has 5 layers: Perception → Network → Middleware → Application → Business.
- [x] **M2M Value Chain**: Vendors → Connectivity → Solutions → ASPs → Customer.
- [x] **MQTT** = TCP, Pub/Sub, broker-based, 3 QoS levels, ports 1883/8883.
- [x] **CoAP** = UDP, REST-like, CONF/ NON-conf, ports 5683/5684, RFC 7252.
- [x] **AMQP** = TCP, enterprise-grade, RabbitMQ, ports 5672/5671.
- [x] **DDS** = brokerless, real-time, OMG standard.
- [x] **LwM2M** = device management protocol built on CoAP (OMA).
- [x] **HTTP/REST** = heavy, NOT ideal for constrained nodes.
- [x] **OneM2M** = global standard for common service layer & interoperability.
- [x] **NB-IoT** bandwidth = 180 kHz; LTE-M for higher mobility.
- [x] **LoRaWAN SF range** = SF7 (fastest) to SF12 (longest range, slowest).
- [x] **Shannon capacity** formula: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — memorize.
- [x] **FSPL formula** increases with $20 \log(d) + 20 \log(f)$.
- [x] **PDR** = received/transmitted packets.
- [x] **ETSI TS 102 690** is the original M2M standard; **OneM2M TS 118 101** is the evolved standard.
- [x] **3GPP Machine-Type Communications (MTC)** standardized from **Release 10**.
- [x] **M2M Standards Bodies**: ETSI, 3GPP, OMA, IEEE, OneM2M.
- [x] **IoT vs IoE**: IoE = People + Process + Data + Things (Cisco terminology).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Application Areas of IoT Technology (Case Studies of at Least Four Domains) - Energy Management and Smart Grid, IoT for Home, Cities, Environment Monitoring, Agriculture, Supply Chain and Customer Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET634 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Typical Application Areas of IoT Technology (Case Studies of at Least Four Domains) - Energy Management and Smart Grid, IoT for Home, Cities, Environment Monitoring, Agriculture, Supply Chain and Customer Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a dynamic, globally interoperable network infrastructure where physical and virtual "Things" — uniquely identifiable entities equipped with sensing, actuation, communication, and data-processing capabilities — cooperate to deliver intelligent applications and services across diverse application domains. Per the **ITU-T Y.2060 Recommendation**, IoT extends connectivity beyond traditional Human-to-Human (H2H) and Human-to-Computer (H2C) interactions to include **Machine-to-Machine (M2M)** communications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Exact):** An IoT application domain is a **vertical industry segment** where networked smart objects deliver domain-specific services. The seven canonical domains per the syllabus are: (1) Energy & Smart Grid, (2) Home, (3) Cities, (4) Environment Monitoring, (5) Agriculture, (6) Supply Chain, and (7) Customer Monitoring.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **human nervous system**:
- **Sensors** = nerve endings in skin (feel pain, temperature)
- **Network/Connectivity** = spinal cord (transmits signals)
- **Cloud/Processing** = brain (decides what to do)
- **Actuators** = muscles (execute response)
- **Application Domains** = different organs (heart-lung, digestive, etc.) — each organ uses the same nervous system but performs a unique function.

Similarly, IoT reuses the same basic "sense → transmit → analyze → act" pipeline across seven different "organs" (application domains).

### 1.3 The Seven Application Domains — Snapshot

| # | Domain | Primary Goal | Key IoT Value |
|---|--------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Energy & Smart Grid** | Optimize generation, distribution, consumption | **30%** reduction in peak load via demand-response |
| 2 | **Home (Smart Home)** | Automate domestic comfort & safety | **25%** energy savings (BIS standard) |
| 3 | **Cities (Smart City)** | Improve urban services | Real-time traffic/pollution management |
| 4 | **Environment Monitoring** | Track ecological parameters | Early-warning for disasters (ppm-level sensing) |
| 5 | **Agriculture (Smart Farming)** | Maximize yield, minimize input | **Up to 90%** water savings via precision irrigation |
| 6 | **Supply Chain** | End-to-end asset traceability | Real-time cold-chain integrity (FDA 21 CFR Part 11) |
| 7 | **Customer Monitoring** | Personalize retail/healthcare services | Behavioural analytics with consent (DPDP Act 2023) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** The global number of connected IoT devices crossed **18.8 billion in 2024** (IoT Analytics), with **60%** deployed across these seven application verticals. Smart Energy + Smart Cities together account for **~35%** of deployments.

### 1.4 IoT Reference Architecture (Domain-Agnostic)

Every application domain — whether home or agriculture — is built on the same **5-layer IoT architecture**:

$$ \text{Perception Layer} \rightarrow \text{Network Layer} \rightarrow \text{Edge/Processing Layer} \rightarrow \text{Application Layer} \rightarrow \text{Business Layer} $$

- **Perception:** Physical sensors/actuators (the "Things")
- **Network:** Connectivity (Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN, Zigbee, NB-IoT, 5G)
- **Edge/Processing:** Fog/edge computing for latency-critical decisions
- **Application:** Domain-specific services (smart meters, irrigation, etc.)
- **Business:** Analytics, monetization, regulatory compliance

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When asked "Explain any IoT application domain," always frame your answer using these **5 layers**. Toppers always anchor their explanation to this canonical model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Domain 1: IoT for Energy Management and Smart Grid

A **Smart Grid** is an electricity network that integrates IoT, bidirectional communication, and advanced metering infrastructure (AMI) to optimize power generation, distribution, and consumption in real time.

#### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):

1. **Sensing:** Smart meters (IEC 62056 standard) record consumption at **15-minute intervals** (compared to monthly manual reads in legacy grids).
2. **Aggregation:** Data Concentrator Units (DCU) collect readings from **~500–2000 smart meters** per cluster.
3. **Communication:** Power Line Communication (PLC) or RF mesh backhauls to the **Head-End System (HES)**.
4. **Analytics:** MDMS (Meter Data Management System) applies **load forecasting, theft detection, and Time-of-Use (ToU) tariff** logic.
5. **Actuation:** Automated Demand Response (ADR) signals trigger load-shedding in non-critical appliances.

#### Mathematical Foundations (High-Yield Equations):

**2.1.1 Peak-to-Average Ratio (PAR) Reduction** — measures how much IoT-based demand response smooths the load curve:

$$ \text{PAR} = \frac{P_{\text{peak}}}{P_{\text{avg}}} $$

For a traditional grid, PAR ≈ 2.5; with smart grid, target PAR ≤ **1.5**.

**2.1.2 Transformer Loading Efficiency** — IoT monitors distribution transformers in real time:

$$ \eta_{\text{tx}} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{out}} + P_{\text{cu}} + P_{\text{core}}} \times 100\% $$

where $P_{\text{cu}} = I^2 R$ (copper losses) and $P_{\text{core}}$ (iron losses) are monitored via IoT temperature/vibration sensors.

**2.1.3 Demand Response Benefit** — energy cost saving per household:

$$ \text{Savings} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left( E_t \times (R_{\text{peak},t} - R_{\text{off-peak},t}) \right) $$

where $E_t$ is energy consumed in interval $t$, and $R$ is the tariff rate.

**2.1.4 Line Loss Calculation** — IoT sensors compute technical losses in feeders:

$$ L_{\text{line}} \ (\%) = \frac{P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100 $$

In India, AT&C (Aggregate Technical & Commercial) losses were **~20%** in 2023; smart grid target is **<12%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Standards to Memorize:**
> - **IEC 62056:** Data exchange for meter reading
> - **IEEE 2030:** Smart Grid interoperability
> - **IS 16444:** Indian standard for smart meters
> - **OpenADR 2.0b:** Demand response protocol

---

### 2.2 Domain 2: IoT for Smart Home

A **Smart Home** integrates sensors, actuators, and intelligent controllers to provide remote monitoring and automation of lighting, climate, security, and entertainment.

#### Subsystem Architecture:

| Subsystem | Sensors | Actuators | Protocol (typical) |
|-----------|---------|-----------|-------------------|
| Lighting | LDR, occupancy (PIR) | Smart dimmers, RGB LEDs | Zigbee / BLE |
| Climate | DHT22 (T, RH), CO₂ | Smart thermostat, HVAC | Wi-Fi / Thread |
| Security | PIR, door contact, glass-break | Smart lock, siren, IP cam | Z-Wave / Wi-Fi |
| Safety | Smoke, gas (MQ-2), water leak | Solenoid valve, alarm | Zigbee |
| Entertainment | IR, voice (mic) | Smart TV, speakers | Wi-Fi / IR |

#### High-Yield Formula — Energy Saving via Occupancy-Based Control:

$$ E_{\text{saved}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i \times t_{\text{unocc},i} \times \eta_{\text{control}} $$

where $P_i$ is the rated power of appliance $i$, $t_{\text{unocc},i}$ is the unoccupied time (hours), and $\eta_{\text{control}}$ is the control efficiency (typically **0.85–0.95**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard:** **Matter (formerly Project CHIP)** is the new royalty-free smart home interoperability standard (2022), backed by Apple, Google, Amazon, and the CSA. It unifies Zigbee, Thread, Wi-Fi, and BLE under a single IP-based protocol — important for KTU 2024.

#### Case Study — Google Nest Thermostat:

- Uses **9 sensors** (temp, humidity, proximity, ambient light, IR, etc.)
- Edge AI (on-device) builds a **thermal model** of the house in ~7 days.
- Reports **10–12% heating** and **15% cooling** bill reduction (Nest Labs data).

---

### 2.3 Domain 3: IoT for Smart Cities

A **Smart City** uses city-wide IoT infrastructure to improve the quality of urban services — transportation, utilities, public safety, and governance.

#### Core Sub-Domains:

1. **Smart Traffic:** Adaptive signal control (SCATS, SCOOT) using loop + video + radar sensors. Formula for green-time optimization:
$$ G_{\text{opt}} = \frac{L \times C}{S \times n} $$
where $L$ = lost time, $C$ = cycle time, $S$ = saturation flow, $n$ = number of phases.

2. **Smart Parking:** Ground sensors (magnetometer, ultrasonic) detect occupancy; mobile app directs driver to nearest free slot. Reduces cruising time by **~30%**.

3. **Smart Street Lighting:** LED lamps with embedded LDR + PIR dim to **30% brightness** when no motion is detected, saving **~70% energy**.

4. **Smart Waste Management:** Ultrasonic fill-level sensors on bins trigger collection only when threshold (e.g., 80%) is reached, reducing trips by **~40%**.

#### Key Standard: **ISO 37120 — Sustainable Cities Indicators**

---

### 2.4 Domain 4: IoT for Environment Monitoring

#### Sensor Suite (KTU-favorite):

| Parameter | Sensor | Range | Accuracy |
|-----------|--------|-------|----------|
| PM2.5/PM10 | Laser scattering (SDS011) | 0–1000 µg/m³ | ±10% |
| CO₂ | NDIR (MH-Z19) | 0–5000 ppm | ±50 ppm |
| SO₂ / NO₂ | Electrochemical (Alphasense) | 0–20 ppm | ±2% |
| Temperature | PT100 / DHT22 | -40 to +125 °C | ±0.5 °C |
| Water pH | Glass electrode | 0–14 pH | ±0.02 pH |
| Dissolved O₂ | Optical (optode) | 0–20 mg/L | ±0.1 mg/L |

#### High-Yield Formula — Air Quality Index (Indian AQI, CPCB):

$$ \text{AQI} = \frac{\text{AQI}_{\text{high}} - \text{AQI}_{\text{low}}}{C_{\text{high}} - C_{\text{low}}} \times (C_i - C_{\text{low}}) + \text{AQI}_{\text{low}} $$

where $C_i$ is the pollutant concentration, and (low/high) are the breakpoint concentrations. Indian AQI bands: **0–50 Good, 51–100 Satisfactory, 101–200 Moderate, 201–300 Poor, 301–400 Very Poor, 401–500 Severe**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case Study — IBM Green Horizons (Beijing):** Deployed a network of ground + satellite + traffic IoT sensors. Predicted PM2.5 with **~75% accuracy** 72 hours in advance, enabling city-wide advisories.

---

### 2.5 Domain 5: IoT for Agriculture (Smart Farming)

#### Core Subsystems:

1. **Precision Irrigation:** Soil moisture (volumetric water content, VWC) + weather forecast + evapotranspiration (ET) models trigger drip irrigation. Formula:
$$ \text{ET}_c = K_c \times \text{ET}_0 $$
where $K_c$ is crop coefficient, $\text{ET}_0$ is reference evapotranspiration (Penman-Monteith, FAO-56). Smart systems save **30–50%** water versus flood irrigation.

2. **Precision Fertilization:** NPK sensors, leaf chlorophyll (SPAD), and aerial drone multispectral imaging compute the **NDVI**:
$$ \text{NDVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}} $$
NDVI values: bare soil ≈ 0.1, sparse vegetation ≈ 0.2–0.5, dense canopy ≈ 0.7–0.9.

3. **Greenhouse Automation:** Micro-climate control with actuators for ventilation, shading, heating, CO₂ enrichment. Uses **LoRaWAN** (2–3 km range, 10+ year battery) for field sensors.

4. **Livestock Monitoring:** Wearable GPS + accelerometer + temperature tags detect estrus, lameness, and disease onset 24–48 hours before clinical symptoms.

---

### 2.6 Domain 6: IoT for Supply Chain Management

#### Cold-Chain Monitoring (KTU-relevant):
Perishable goods (vaccines, dairy, seafood) must maintain **2–8 °C** (vaccines) or **−18 °C** (frozen). IoT tags continuously log:
- Temperature (±0.2 °C)
- Humidity (±2% RH)
- GPS location
- Door open/close events
- Shock (g-force > 4G)

**HACCP Critical Limit Violation:**

$$ \text{Violation} = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} (T(t) - T_{\text{set}}) \, dt > \text{Vaccine Threshold} $$

**GS1 EPC Class 1 Gen 2 (UHF RFID)** is the de-facto global standard — tag ID is 96 bits, supports up to **1000 tags/sec** in dense reader mode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Study — Maersk TradeLens (now discontinued 2023):** Used Hyperledger Fabric blockchain + IoT container tracking. Reduced shipping document processing time from **5 days to under 2 hours** in pilot runs.

---

### 2.7 Domain 7: IoT for Customer Monitoring

Two primary use-cases:

**A. Retail — In-Store Analytics:** BLE beacons, smart shelves (load-cell + RFID), and computer vision (people counting) measure dwell time, conversion rate, heat maps.

**B. Healthcare — Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM):** Wearables (ECG, SpO₂, BP cuff, glucometer) stream vitals to a clinician dashboard with alerting thresholds. Standards: **HL7 FHIR** for data exchange, **ISO/IEEE 11073** for personal health device communication.

#### High-Yield Formula — Customer Churn Prediction (Logistic Regression ML layer):

$$ P(\text{churn} \mid x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \cdots + \beta_n x_n)}} $$

The IoT layer (X) feeds behavioural features (login frequency, support calls, app crashes) into this model.

> [!WARNING]
> **Legal Pitfall (India):** Per the **Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act 2023**, customer biometric/physiological data is "personal data" and requires explicit consent. Always mention consent + anonymization in exam answers.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Single Quick-Reference Table)

| # | Domain | Key Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|--------|-------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Smart Grid | $\text{PAR} = P_{\text{peak}} / P_{\text{avg}}$ | Peak-to-Average Ratio | 1.5 (target) |
| 2 | Smart Grid | $\eta_{\text{tx}} = P_{\text{out}} / (P_{\text{out}} + P_{\text{cu}} + P_{\text{core}})$ | Transformer efficiency | 95–99% |
| 3 | Smart Home | $E_{\text{saved}} = \sum P_i t_{\text{unocc},i} \eta_{\text{control}}$ | Energy saved (Wh) | — |
| 4 | Smart Traffic | $G_{\text{opt}} = L \cdot C / (S \cdot n)$ | Optimal green time (s) | 15–60 s |
| 5 | Environment | AQI breakpoint linear interpolation | Indian AQI | 0–500 |
| 6 | Agriculture | $\text{ET}_c = K_c \cdot \text{ET}_0$ | Crop evapotranspiration | FAO-56 |
| 7 | Agriculture | $\text{NDVI} = (\text{NIR} - \text{Red}) / (\text{NIR} + \text{Red})$ | Vegetation index | −1 to +1 |
| 8 | Supply Chain | $\int (T - T_{\text{set}}) \, dt > \text{Limit}$ | Cold-chain violation | Vaccine 2–8 °C |
| 9 | Customer | $P = 1 / (1 + e^{-z})$ | Logistic churn probability | 0–1 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Smart Grid Peak Load Reduction

**Problem:** A residential feeder has 200 houses, each with an average load of **2 kW** and a peak load of **5 kW** for 2 hours in the evening. The utility introduces IoT-based demand response that shifts **40%** of the peak load to off-peak hours. Compute (a) PAR before, (b) PAR after, (c) Daily energy saved in monetary terms, given the ToU tariff: **Peak = ₹8/kWh**, **Off-peak = ₹4/kWh**.

**Step 1 — Energy without demand response (baseline):**

$$ E_{\text{base}} = 200 \times \left( 2\,\text{kW} \times 24\,\text{h} - (5 - 2)\,\text{kW} \times 2\,\text{h} \right) $$

$$ E_{\text{base}} = 200 \times (48 - 6) = 200 \times 42 = 8400\ \text{kWh/day} $$

**Step 2 — Baseline cost (assuming 2 peak hours @ ₹8 and 22 off-peak hours @ ₹4):**

$$ C_{\text{base}} = 200 \times \left[ 5 \times 2 \times 8 + 2 \times 22 \times 4 \right] $$

$$ C_{\text{base}} = 200 \times (80 + 176) = 200 \times 256 = ₹51{,}200/\text{day} $$

**Step 3 — PAR baseline:**

$$ \text{PAR}_{\text{base}} = \frac{P_{\text{peak}}}{P_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{5\,\text{kW}}{2\,\text{kW}} = 2.5 $$

**Step 4 — After 40% peak shift:** 40% of 5 kW = **2 kW** shifted; new peak = 3 kW.

$$ \text{PAR}_{\text{new}} = \frac{3\,\text{kW}}{2\,\text{kW}} = 1.5 $$

**Step 5 — New daily cost (peak now 2 hours of 3 kW @ ₹8, off-peak gains 2 kW × 2 h @ ₹4):**

$$ C_{\text{new}} = 200 \times \left[ 3 \times 2 \times 8 + (2 \times 22 + 2 \times 2) \times 4 \right] $$

$$ C_{\text{new}} = 200 \times (48 + 184) = 200 \times 232 = ₹46{,}400/\text{day} $$

**Step 6 — Daily monetary saving:**

$$ \Delta C = C_{\text{base}} - C_{\text{new}} = 51{,}200 - 46{,}400 = \boxed{₹4{,}800/\text{day}} $$

**Step 7 — Annual saving (365 days):**

$$ \Delta C_{\text{annual}} = 4800 \times 365 = \boxed{₹17{,}52{,}000/\text{year}} $$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: NDVI Computation in Smart Agriculture

**Problem:** A drone multispectral camera measures canopy reflectance at Red (band centred at 660 nm) = **0.08** and NIR (band centred at 840 nm) = **0.42**. Compute NDVI and classify crop health.

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**

$$ \text{NDVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}} = \frac{0.42 - 0.08}{0.42 + 0.08} $$

**Step 2 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$ 0.42 - 0.08 = 0.34 $$

**Step 3 — Denominator evaluation:**

$$ 0.42 + 0.08 = 0.50 $$

**Step 4 — Final ratio:**

$$ \text{NDVI} = \frac{0.34}{0.50} = 0.68 $$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** NDVI = 0.68 corresponds to **dense, healthy vegetation** (e.g., mature wheat or paddy). If NDVI < 0.3, it indicates stressed or sparse canopy — farmer should be alerted for irrigation/fertilization.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Cold-Chain Violation in Vaccine Logistics

**Problem:** A refrigerated truck transports vaccines at set-point $T_{\text{set}} = 5\ ^\circ\text{C}$. The IoT temperature log shows:

- **0–1 h:** $T = 5.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- **1–2 h:** $T = 6.5\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- **2–3 h:** $T = 7.5\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- **3–4 h:** $T = 4.5\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (door opened, then closed)
- **4–5 h:** $T = 5.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$

Compute the **temperature-violation integral** and determine if the WHO PQS E001 limit (≥ **10 °C·h** above set-point cumulative) is breached.

**Step 1 — Write the violation integral piecewise:**

$$ V = \int_{0}^{5} \max(T(t) - T_{\text{set}}, 0)\, dt $$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each hour (using midpoint, $\max$ rule):**

| Hour | Mid T (°C) | $T - 5$ | Used (°C·h) |
|------|-----------|---------|-------------|
| 0–1 | 5.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 1–2 | 6.5 | +1.5 | 1.5 |
| 2–3 | 7.5 | +2.5 | 2.5 |
| 3–4 | 4.5 | −0.5 | 0.0 (clipped) |
| 4–5 | 5.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 |

**Step 3 — Sum the violation:**

$$ V = 0 + 1.5 + 2.5 + 0 + 0 = 4.0\ ^\circ\text{C}\cdot\text{h} $$

**Step 4 — Compare to WHO PQS E001 threshold:**

$$ V = 4.0\ ^\circ\text{C}\cdot\text{h} \ <\ 10\ ^\circ\text{C}\cdot\text{h} $$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Vaccines are **still safe**, but a warning alert should be raised because temperature exceeded 5 °C for 2 cumulative hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This exact type of calculation is a **KTU 14-mark question favourite**. Always show the piecewise integration table explicitly.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Seven Application Domains

| Domain | Latency Need | Bandwidth Need | Power Budget | Connectivity | Data Frequency |
|--------|--------------|----------------|--------------|--------------|----------------|
| Energy / Smart Grid | Low (seconds) | Medium | Mains-powered | PLC, RF Mesh, NB-IoT | 15-min intervals |
| Smart Home | Low–Medium | High (video) | AC mains + battery | Wi-Fi, Zigbee, Matter | Event-driven / 1 Hz |
| Smart City | Mixed (ms for traffic) | High (CCTV) | Mostly mains | 4G/5G, Fiber | 1–10 Hz |
| Environment | Low | Low | Battery + solar | LoRaWAN, NB-IoT | 1 per 5–15 min |
| Agriculture | Very Low | Low | Battery (10 yr) | LoRaWAN, Sigfox | 1 per 15–60 min |
| Supply Chain | Low | Low | Battery (RFID) | UHF RFID, BLE, 5G | On-event / hourly |
| Customer Monitoring | Medium (alerts) | Medium | Battery + charger | BLE, Wi-Fi, cellular | 1–100 Hz (vitals) |

---

### 3.5 KTU Model Reasoning Template (Use for Any 14-Mark Question)

When asked to **"Explain IoT in [domain] with a case study"**, structure your answer in **exactly 5 paragraphs**:

1. **Introduction (2 marks):** Definition, scope, importance.
2. **Architecture (3 marks):** 5-layer reference model mapped to that domain.
3. **Sensors & Technologies (3 marks):** Specific sensors, protocols, hardware.
4. **Case Study (3 marks):** A real-world deployment with quantitative outcomes.
5. **Challenges & Future Scope (3 marks):** Security, privacy, scalability, energy, regulations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
