<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between Identification and Addressing in IoT. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Identification | Addressing |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Identifies *what* the object is | Identifies *where* the object is on the network |
| **Layer** | Application layer | Network layer |
| **Standard** | EPC, uCode, RFID tag ID | IPv4, IPv6 |
| **Example** | EPC `30 1234ABCD 56789.0123456789` on a milk carton | IPv4 `192.168.1.10` assigned to the carton's IoT sensor |
| **Analogy** | A person's name | A person's home address |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference:** Identification is unique *within the application's domain* (e.g., supply chain). Addressing is unique *within the network*. They serve different purposes and often coexist for the same physical object.

**(Award: 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for example.)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: List the three main components of an RFID system and state the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **RFID Tag (Transponder)** — A microchip with an antenna attached to the object. Stores the unique identifier (e.g., EPC). It can be *passive* (no battery), *semi-passive*, or *active* (battery-powered). (1 mark)

2. **RFID Reader (Interrogator)** — Emits RF signals and captures the data backscattered from tags within its read range. Forwards the captured ID to the backend. (1 mark)

3. **Backend Middleware / Database** — Receives the tag data from the reader, looks up product information via services like EPCIS and ONS, and presents it to applications. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question Choice A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Explain the structure of an Electronic Product Code (EPC) with a neat diagram. List the various EPC tag classes.**

**Model Solution:**

**EPC Structure (EPC-96 — most common):**

The 96-bit EPC is divided into four hierarchical fields:

| Field | Size (bits) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Header** | 8 | Identifies the EPC version and encoding scheme (e.g., `0x30` = SGTIN) |
| **EPC Manager Number** | 28 | Identifies the organization (manufacturer) that assigned the EPC |
| **Object Class** | 24 | Identifies the type of product (SKU level) |
| **Serial Number** | 36 | Identifies the specific physical instance of the product |

**Diagrammatic representation:**

```
+----------+--------+---------+--------+
|  Header  | EPC    | Object  | Serial |
|  (8 bits)| Manager| Class   | Number |
|          | (28 b) | (24 b)  | (36 b) |
+----------+--------+---------+--------+
|<------- 96 bits total (12 bytes) ------>|
```

**EPC Tag Classes (as per EPCglobal standards):**

| Class | Power | Read Range | Features |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class 0 | Passive | ≤ 1 m | Read-only, factory programmed |
| Class 1 | Passive | ≤ 3 m | Read-only, mass deployment |
| Class 1 Gen 2 | Passive | ≤ 10 m | Industry standard, anti-collision |
| Class 2 | Passive | ≤ 10 m | Read-Write, basic security |
| Class 3 | Semi-passive | ≤ 100 m | Battery-assisted, sensor-capable |
| Class 4 | Active | ≤ 100 m | P2P communication, mini-reader |
| Class 5 | Active | > 100 m | Reader-class, powers other tags |

**(Marks: 3 for structure with diagram, 4 for tag classes table.)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Explain the architecture of the uCode identification system with a block diagram. Compare uCode with EPC.**

**Model Solution:**

**uCode/uID Architecture (developed by Ken Sakamura, Japan):**

The uID architecture has three main components:

1. **uCode** — A 128-bit (standard) or 256-bit (extended) identifier stored on physical tags (RFID, QR, barcode, etc.).

2. **Ubiquitous Communicator (UC)** — A reader device (e.g., mobile phone) that reads the uCode and queries the resolution server for metadata.

3. **uCode Resolution Server** — A globally distributed lookup service that maps a uCode to the location (URL) of the object's metadata (its "digital twin").

**Workflow:** Tag → UC reads uCode → UC queries Resolution Server → Server returns metadata URL → UC fetches and displays object information.

**Comparison Table: uCode vs EPC:**

| Aspect | EPC | uCode |
|---|---|---|
| **Length** | 64 / 96 / 128 bits | 128 (standard), 256 (extended) bits |
| **Origin** | MIT Auto-ID Labs, USA (1999) | T-Engine Forum, Japan (2003) |
| **Primary Use** | Supply chain & retail | Universal ubiquitous computing |
| **Carrier Media** | Mainly passive UHF RFID | RFID, QR, barcode, NFC, infrared (medium-independent) |
| **Resolution Mechanism** | ONS (Object Name Service, DNS-based) | Distributed Hash Table (DHT) |
| **Hierarchical Structure** | Header + Manager + Class + Serial | Flat 128-bit identifier |
| **Standardization Body** | EPCglobal / GS1 | YRP UNL / uID Center |

**(Marks: 4 for architecture + diagram, 3 for comparison table.)**

---

### **Question Choice B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Compare IPv4 and IPv6 addressing schemes with respect to: address length, header size, address space, security, auto-configuration, and quality of service.**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | IPv4 | IPv6 |
|---|---|---|
| **Address Length** | 32 bits | 128 bits |
| **Total Address Space** | $2^{32} \approx 4.29 \times 10^{9}$ | $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ |
| **Notation** | Dotted-decimal (192.168.1.1) | Colon-hexadecimal (2001:db8::1) |
| **Header Size** | Variable (20–60 bytes) | Fixed (40 bytes) |
| **Fragmentation** | Both routers & sender | Sender only |
| **Broadcast** | Supported | **Not supported** (uses multicast/anycast) |
| **Security (IPsec)** | Optional | Mandatory support |
| **Auto-configuration** | Requires DHCP server | Built-in SLAAC |
| **QoS Support** | Limited (ToS field) | Built-in Flow Label |
| **Header Checksum** | Present (every hop verifies) | Removed (faster routing) |
| **Examples of Address Types** | Public, Private (RFC 1918), Multicast | Global Unicast, Link-Local (`fe80::/10`), ULA, Multicast, Anycast |
| **Mobility Support** | Limited | Better support (Mobile IPv6) |

**Justification for IoT Adoption of IPv6:**

1. **Massive address space** — required for billions of IoT devices.
2. **Stateless auto-configuration (SLAAC)** — IoT nodes can self-configure without DHCP.
3. **Mandatory IPsec** — secures IoT communications at the network layer.
4. **Smaller routing tables** — hierarchical aggregation.
5. **6LoWPAN adaptation** — IPv6 over IEEE 802.15.4 LPWAN networks.

**(Marks: 5 for table, 2 for IoT-specific justification.)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q: With an example, explain IPv6 address notation. An organization is assigned the IPv6 block `2001:db8:abcd::/48`. Design sub-blocks for 4 departments using `/64` prefixes and list the resulting sub-block addresses.**

**Model Solution:**

**IPv6 Address Notation Rules:**

1. **Full Form:** Eight groups of 4 hexadecimal digits, each group representing 16 bits.
   ```
   2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334
   ```

2. **Rule 1 — Leading Zeros:** Leading zeros within a group may be omitted.
   ```
   0db8  →  db8
   0370  →  370
   ```

3. **Rule 2 — Double Colon (`::`):** A single contiguous run of all-zero groups can be replaced by `::`. Used only ONCE in an address.
   ```
   2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001  →  2001:db8::1
   ```

**Sub-block Design from `2001:db8:abcd::/48` to /64:**

A `/48` prefix has 16 bits available for subnetting within the organization. We need to allocate `/64` prefixes to 4 departments.

**Step 1 — Determine required subnet bits:**
$$
n = 64 - 48 = 16 \text{ bits available for subnetting}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine bits needed for 4 departments:**
$$
2^{k} \geq 4 \implies k = 2 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct sub-blocks** (using the first 2 bits of the 16-bit subnet ID):

| Dept | Subnet ID (16-bit hex) | Resulting /64 Block |
|---|---|---|
| **Dept 1 (Admin)** | `0000` | `2001:db8:abcd:0000::/64` |
| **Dept 2 (R&D)** | `0001` | `2001:db8:abcd:0001::/64` |
| **Dept 3 (Sales)** | `0002` | `2001:db8:abcd:0002::/64` |
| **Dept 4 (IoT Lab)** | `0003` | `2001:db8:abcd:0003::/64` |

**Compressed representations:**
- Admin: `2001:db8:abcd::/64`
- R&D: `2001:db8:abcd:1::/64`
- Sales: `2001:db8:abcd:2::/64`
- IoT Lab: `2001:db8:abcd:3::/64`

**Verification — Number of addresses per /64 block:**
$$
A(64) = 2^{128-64} = 2^{64} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{19}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for students:** In IPv6, the typical convention is to allocate `/64` blocks to LANs. The 64-bit host portion (interface ID) is then automatically derived from the device's MAC address via SLAAC (EUI-64 conversion).

**(Marks: 3 for notation rules + examples, 4 for sub-block design with calculations.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing EPC with RFID:** EPC is just one identification *scheme* that can be stored *on* an RFID tag. RFID is the *carrier technology* (radio waves); EPC is the *numbering scheme* (the bits stored). Marks are deducted when students treat them as synonyms.
> 2. **Writing `::` more than once in an IPv6 address:** This is a syntax error. The `::` can appear *at most once* to maintain address uniqueness.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract 2 in IPv4 subnetting:** Network address and broadcast address are *not* usable for hosts — students frequently forget this.
> 4. **Mixing up identification vs addressing:** Identification answers "WHAT is it?" Addressing answers "WHERE is it on the network?". Examiners specifically look for this distinction in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Missing the `fe80::/10` Link-Local scope:** IPv6 link-local addresses are auto-configured on every IPv6 interface and are *not routable* beyond the local link. Many students incorrectly assume all IPv6 addresses are globally routable.
> 6. **Forgetting mandatory IPsec in IPv6:** IPv6 was designed with security as a mandatory feature from inception; confusing this with IPv4's optional IPsec is a common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Keep this in mind for the last 30 minutes of revision.**

### Identification Layer (Application Layer IDs)

- **EPC (Electronic Product Code)** — Unique ID for physical items in the supply chain. Originated at MIT Auto-ID Labs (1999). Most common form: **96 bits**, structured into **Header (8) + Manager (28) + Class (24) + Serial (36)**.
- **RFID (Radio-Frequency Identification)** — Wireless auto-ID technology. **Three components**: Tag (transponder), Reader (interrogator), Middleware (backend). Uses **backscatter modulation** for passive tags.
- **uCode (Ubiquitous Code)** — 128-bit ID for *any* physical/virtual object. **Medium-independent** (works with RFID, QR, barcode). Uses **DHT-based resolution** via the **uID architecture (Sakamura, Japan)**.
- **Comparison:** EPC ⊂ RFID world; uCode is broader, medium-independent; both map to metadata via ONS / Resolution Server (analogous to DNS).

### Network Layer (Addressing)

- **IPv4** — 32-bit, ~4.29 billion addresses, dotted-decimal notation (`192.168.1.10`), variable header (20–60 bytes). Classful addressing (A/B/C/D/E) is historical; modern networks use **CIDR (`/M`)**.
- **IPv6** — 128-bit, ~$3.4 \times 10^{38}$ addresses, colon-hex notation (`2001:db8::1`), **fixed 40-byte header**. **No broadcast** (uses multicast/anycast). **Mandatory IPsec**. **SLAAC** for auto-configuration.
- **6LoWPAN** — Adaptation layer enabling IPv6 over **IEEE 802.15.4** (Zigbee, Thread). Compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header down to as low as **2–7 bytes** (saving up to **~87.5%** overhead).

### Key Formulas to Memorize

- IPv4 total addresses: $\;N_{v4} = 2^{32}$
- IPv4 usable hosts per subnet: $\;H = 2^{32-M} - 2$
- IPv6 total addresses: $\;N_{v6} = 2^{128}$
- IPv6 addresses per /P prefix: $\;A(P) = 2^{128-P}$
- EPC-96 total unique IDs: $\;N_{EPC} = 2^{96}$
- 6LoWPAN compression gain: $\;G = \dfrac{40 - H_{6L}}{40} \times 100\%$

### Exam-Day Reminders

1. **Identification ≠ Addressing** — always clarify which layer is being discussed.
2. **EPC ≠ RFID** — EPC is the *number*; RFID is the *technology* that carries it.
3. **`::` appears at most ONCE** in any IPv6 address — and only for a *contiguous* run of zero groups.
4. **IPv4 reserves 2 addresses per subnet** (network + broadcast); IPv6 does **NOT** have broadcast — no equivalent reservation.
5. **Always show the formula** before plugging in numbers — KTU examiners award partial credit for correct methodology even if the final answer has an arithmetic slip.
6. **IoT needs IPv6 + 6LoWPAN + EPC/uCode** for scalable, end-to-end unique identification and addressing.

### Cross-Module Connections

- **Module 1 (IoT Architecture):** Identification & addressing fit into the *Perception* layer (sensors/RFID tags) and *Network* layer (IPv4/v6).
- **Module 3 onward (IoT Protocols):** MQTT, CoAP, HTTP all rely on IP addressing at the network layer.
- **Module 5 (IoT Security):** IPsec (IPv6), RFID authentication (EPC Gen 2 passwords) — both topics connect back here.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET634 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors and Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Sensor?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Sensor** is an electronic device (or transducer) that detects physical, chemical, or biological quantities from the environment (such as **temperature**, **pressure**, **light**, **humidity**, **motion**, or **gas concentration**) and converts these non-electrical physical stimuli into a corresponding measurable **electrical signal** (voltage, current, resistance, or frequency) that can be processed, displayed, or transmitted.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body
Think of sensors as the **five senses of an IoT system**, just like a human body:
- Your **skin** is a *temperature* and *pressure sensor* → it detects heat and touch.
- Your **eyes** are *light sensors* → they convert photons (light) into neural signals (electrical impulses) for the brain.
- Your **ears** are *acoustic sensors* → they convert sound waves into electrical nerve impulses.

In an IoT system, sensors play this exact role — they are the **"eyes, ears, and skin"** that allow a machine to *perceive* the physical world, which it otherwise cannot understand. The detected signal is then sent to a **microcontroller** (the "brain") for processing.

## 1.2 What is an Actuator?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> An **Actuator** is a device that operates in the **opposite direction** of a sensor. It takes an **electrical signal** (command from a controller) as input and converts it into a corresponding **physical action or output** such as motion, heat, light, or force. In simple words, actuators *make things happen* in the real world.

### Conceptual Analogy — Muscles of the IoT System
If sensors are the **senses**, then actuators are the **muscles** of an IoT system. For example:
- A signal from a microcontroller → an electric **motor rotates** (actuator) → a fan blade spins.
- A signal → a **solenoid valve opens** → water flows.
- A signal → an **LED glows** → light is emitted.

The sensor **observes**, the controller **decides**, and the actuator **acts** — completing the fundamental perception–cognition–action loop in IoT.

## 1.3 The Sensor–Actuator Pair in an IoT Loop

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:**
> In any IoT application, sensors and actuators are **never used in isolation**. They form the **Physical Layer (Perception Layer)** of the standard **5-layer IoT architecture** and work in closed loops with a microcontroller/MCU. Together, they convert the physical world into data (sensing) and data back into physical action (actuation).

| Component | Direction | Energy Conversion | Human Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensor** | Environment → System | Physical quantity → Electrical signal | Eyes, ears, skin |
| **Actuator** | System → Environment | Electrical signal → Physical action | Muscles, hands, legs |
| **Microcontroller** | Bidirectional | Processing & decision-making | Brain |

## 1.4 Classification of Sensors (KTU 2024 Syllabus Emphasis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensors are broadly classified into TWO major categories** that KTU examiners love to test:

### A. Based on Power Requirement
- **Active Sensors**: Generate their own electrical output signal from the physical input. *No external power needed for transduction.*  
  *Examples:* Photovoltaic cell (solar cell), piezoelectric sensor, thermocouple, RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector).

- **Passive Sensors**: Require an **external power source (excitation voltage)** to operate. The physical quantity merely *modulates* an external signal.  
  *Examples:* Thermistor, LDR (Light Dependent Resistor), strain gauge, potentiometer, capacitive humidity sensor.

### B. Based on Output Signal Type
- **Analog Sensors**: Produce a **continuous output signal** proportional to the input.  
  *Examples:* LM35 temperature sensor, MQ-series gas sensors, flex sensor.
- **Digital Sensors**: Produce a **discrete (binary or digital word)** output.  
  *Examples:* DHT11/DHT22 (single-wire digital protocol), DS18B20 (1-Wire), MPU6050 (I²C), ultrasonic HC-SR04 (digital pulse).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** A sensor's effectiveness is measured using the **Sensitivity (S)** in units of **V/unit (input)**, **A/unit (input)**, or **Ω/unit (input)**. Sensitivity is one of the most important parameters tested in KTU exams.

## 1.5 Classification of Actuators

### A. Based on Energy Domain
- **Electrical Actuators** — DC/Stepper/Servo motors, relays, solenoids.
- **Hydraulic Actuators** — Cylinders, hydraulic motors (use liquid pressure).
- **Pneumatic Actuators** — Air cylinders, pneumatic valves (use compressed air).
- **Thermal Actuators** — Heating elements, Peltier modules.
- **Mechanical Actuators** — Gears, levers, cams, springs.
- **Optical Actuators** — LEDs, laser diodes, LCD/OLED displays.
- **Chemical Actuators** — Drug delivery micropumps, pH-modulating systems.

### B. Based on Motion Type
- **Linear Actuators** — Produce straight-line motion (e.g., solenoid, linear motor).
- **Rotary Actuators** — Produce rotational motion (e.g., DC motor, servo motor).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Key Performance Characteristics of Sensors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite Question Area:** Examiners frequently ask for definitions and significance of at least 5–6 sensor characteristics. Memorize these with their standard units.

| S.No | Characteristic | Formal Definition | Standard Unit / Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Accuracy** | Closeness of the measured value to the *true value*. | Percentage (%) or Error % |
| 2 | **Precision** | Closeness of repeated measurements to *each other* (repeatability). | Standard Deviation (σ) |
| 3 | **Sensitivity (S)** | Ratio of change in output to change in input. | V/°C, mV/kPa, Ω/°C |
| 4 | **Resolution** | Smallest detectable change in input. | °C, Pa, m, bits |
| 5 | **Range** | Minimum and maximum input values the sensor can measure. | Min–Max (e.g., −40 °C to +125 °C) |
| 6 | **Linearity** | How closely the output curve follows a straight line. | Non-linearity % |
| 7 | **Hysteresis** | Difference in output for the same input when approached from *increasing* vs. *decreasing* direction. | % of Full Scale (FS) |
| 8 | **Response Time (τ)** | Time taken by the sensor output to reach 63.2 % of its final value (1 time-constant). | Seconds (s) or milliseconds (ms) |
| 9 | **Bandwidth** | Range of frequencies over which the sensor responds accurately. | Hz |
| 10 | **Drift** | Slow change of output with time even when input is constant. | Units / hour or / year |

## 2.2 Mathematical Framework of Sensor Behaviour

A real-world sensor is modeled as a **first-order linear time-invariant (LTI) system** in most KTU numerical problems.

### Step 1 — Static (DC) Transfer Function
The static input-output relation of a sensor is:
$$
y(t) = S \cdot x(t) + b
$$
where:
- $y(t)$ = output signal (V or mA)
- $x(t)$ = input physical quantity (°C, Pa, lux, etc.)
- $S$ = **Sensitivity** (slope, units of output/input)
- $b$ = **Offset** (output when input is zero, V)

### Step 2 — Dynamic (Transient) Step Response
When a sudden step input is applied, the output follows an **exponential rise** governed by the time constant $\tau$:
$$
y(t) = y_{\infty} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)
$$
where:
- $y_{\infty}$ = final steady-state output
- $\tau = R \cdot C$ for many thermal/electrical sensor equivalents (seconds)

### Step 3 — Total Error
$$
\text{Total Error} = \text{Systematic Error} + \text{Random Error}
$$
$$
\%\text{Error} = \left\vert \frac{\text{Measured Value} - \text{True Value}}{\text{True Value}} \right\vert \times 100\%
$$

## 2.3 Actuator Characteristics (KTU High-Yield)

| S.No | Characteristic | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stroke** | Maximum displacement produced (mm for linear, ° for rotary) |
| 2 | **Force / Torque** | Force in Newtons (N) or Torque in N·m |
| 3 | **Speed** | Rotational speed (RPM) or linear speed (mm/s) |
| 4 | **Power Rating** | Maximum power the actuator consumes (W or kW) |
| 5 | **Efficiency (η)** | Ratio of useful mechanical output power to electrical input power |
| 6 | **Duty Cycle** | Fraction of time actuator is ON (e.g., 50 % duty cycle) |
| 7 | **Operating Voltage / Current** | Supply rating (e.g., 5 V, 12 V, 230 V AC) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they cover ~80 % of numerical / derivation questions on Sensors and Actuators.**

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Slope of input-output curve |
| % Error | $\%\text{Err} = \dfrac{\vert V_m - V_t \vert}{V_t} \times 100$ | Accuracy calculation |
| Non-linearity | $\%\text{NL} = \dfrac{\Delta y_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Worst deviation from best-fit line |
| Hysteresis | $\%\text{Hys} = \dfrac{y_{up} - y_{down}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Difference in up vs. down readings |
| Time-constant (1st order) | $y(t) = y_{\infty}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Exponential step response |
| 63.2 % response | $y(\tau) = 0.632\, y_{\infty}$ | Defines time-constant |
| Resolution | $R = \dfrac{\text{FS range}}{2^n}$ | For n-bit ADC: $R = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ |
| ADC Conversion | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor$ | Quantized digital output |
| Thermistor (NTC) | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$ | Steinhart-Hart simplified form |
| Thermocouple (Seebeck) | $V = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | α = Seebeck coefficient (V/°C) |
| RTD (Linear approx.) | $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ | α ≈ 0.00385 /°C for Platinum |
| Strain Gauge | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | G = Gauge factor, ε = strain |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not just academic — they are the **design equations** used by embedded systems engineers when selecting sensors for medical devices (e.g., blood pressure sensors), automotive ECUs (e.g., MAP sensors), industrial SCADA systems, and smart agriculture IoT nodes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Sensor Step Response

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is a KTU 2024 frequently-asked Part-B question.** Understand every step clearly.

### Step 1 — Governing Differential Equation
A first-order sensor is modeled as an RC low-pass system. The input is $x(t)$ and the output is $y(t)$:
$$
\tau \cdot \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)
$$
where $\tau$ is the **time-constant** and $K$ is the **static gain** (sensitivity $S$).

### Step 2 — Apply Step Input
A step input of magnitude $A$ is applied at $t = 0$:
$$
x(t) = A \cdot u(t) \quad \text{where } u(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1, & t \geq 0 \end{cases}
$$

### Step 3 — Steady-State Solution (Particular Integral)
At $t \to \infty$, $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = 0$, so:
$$
y_{\infty} = K \cdot A
$$

### Step 4 — Transient Solution (Homogeneous Solution)
$$
y_h(t) = C \cdot e^{-t/\tau}
$$

### Step 5 — Complete Solution
$$
y(t) = y_{\infty} + C \cdot e^{-t/\tau} = K A + C \cdot e^{-t/\tau}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Initial Condition
Assume $y(0) = 0$ (zero initial output). Substituting $t = 0$:
$$
0 = K A + C \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = -K A
$$

### Step 7 — Final Result
$$
\boxed{\,y(t) = K A \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)\,}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This exponential rise tells engineers how *fast* a sensor reacts. A **smaller $\tau$** means a **faster** sensor — critical for real-time IoT applications like airbag deployment (crash sensors) or pacemakers.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem:** A thermistor has a sensitivity of **−2.5 Ω/°C** at 25 °C. The nominal resistance at 25 °C is **10 000 Ω**. If the temperature rises to **85 °C**, calculate:
> (a) Change in resistance. (b) New resistance. (c) % Error if the true resistance is **7 600 Ω**.

### Part (a) — Change in Resistance
$$
\Delta T = 85 - 25 = 60\ \text{°C}
$$
$$
\Delta R = S \cdot \Delta T = (-2.5\ \Omega /°\text{C}) \times 60\ °\text{C} = -150\ \Omega
$$
*(Negative sign indicates NTC — resistance decreases with temperature.)*

### Part (b) — New Resistance
$$
R_{new} = R_{25} + \Delta R = 10000 - 150 = 9850\ \Omega
$$

### Part (c) — Percentage Error
$$
\%\text{Error} = \left\vert \frac{9850 - 7600}{7600} \right\vert \times 100
$$
$$
\%\text{Error} = \left\vert \frac{2250}{7600} \right\vert \times 100
$$
$$
\%\text{Error} = 0.2960 \times 100 = 29.61\ \%
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sensor vs. Actuator

> [!NOTE]
> **This matrix is exam-ready** — KTU examiners expect students to clearly contrast sensors and actuators in 2-mark short questions.

| Parameter | **Sensor** | **Actuator** |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of energy flow | Environment → System | System → Environment |
| Transduction | Physical → Electrical | Electrical → Physical |
| Typical output | Voltage, current, resistance, frequency | Motion, force, heat, light |
| Power role | Usually signal-level (low power) | Usually power-level (high power) |
| Control loop role | Feedback element | Control element / Final control element |
| Example (IoT smart home) | DHT11 (temp + humidity) | Relay + Fan motor |
| Example (Industrial IoT) | Pressure transmitter (4–20 mA) | Pneumatic control valve |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common IoT Sensors (2024 Syllabus)

| Sensor | Physical Quantity | Type | Output | Typical IoT Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DHT11 / DHT22** | Temperature + Humidity | Digital (single-wire) | Digital word | Weather stations, smart ACs |
| **DS18B20** | Temperature | Digital (1-Wire) | 9–12 bit digital | Cold-chain monitoring |
| **LM35** | Temperature | Analog | 10 mV/°C | Industrial panel meters |
| **MQ-2 / MQ-135** | Gas (LPG, CO, smoke) | Analog + digital pin | V (0–5 V) | Fire alarm, air-quality |
| **HC-SR04** | Distance (ultrasonic) | Active digital | Pulse width (μs) | Water level, parking sensors |
| **PIR (HC-SR501)** | Motion (IR) | Digital (TTL) | HIGH/LOW | Intruder detection |
| **MPU6050** | Acceleration + Gyro | Digital (I²C) | 16-bit words | Drones, wearables, fall detection |
| **LDR / Photodiode** | Light intensity | Analog | Resistance / V | Automatic street lights |
| **BMP180** | Pressure + Altitude | Digital (I²C) | 16–19 bit | Altimeter, weather stations |
| **Soil Moisture Sensor** | Water content | Analog | V (0–5 V) | Smart agriculture / irrigation |
| **Flex Sensor** | Bend angle | Analog (resistive) | Resistance change | Robotic fingers, gloves |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common IoT Actuators

| Actuator | Energy Domain | Control Signal | IoT Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Motor** | Electrical → Mechanical | PWM from MCU | Robotic wheels, fans |
| **Servo Motor (SG90)** | Electrical → Angular position | PWM (50 Hz, 1–2 ms pulse) | Robotic arms, camera gimbal |
| **Stepper Motor (28BYJ-48)** | Electrical → Precise steps | 4-phase pulses | 3D printers, CNC |
| **Relay (5V/12V coil)** | Electrical → Electromagnetic switch | Digital HIGH/LOW | Smart home appliance control |
| **Solenoid Valve** | Electrical → Fluid control | 12V/24V pulse | Water irrigation, gas valves |
| **Buzzer (Piezo)** | Electrical → Sound | PWM tone | Alarms, feedback indicators |
| **LED / RGB LED** | Electrical → Light | Digital or PWM | Status indication, smart lighting |
| **Heating Element** | Electrical → Heat | Relay or MOSFET | Smart kettle, incubator |
| **Peltier (TEC)** | Electrical → Temperature change | PWM H-bridge | Portable cooler, PCR machines |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Sensor-Actuator Loop in IoT

> [!NOTE]
> **This block-level architecture is what KTU expects** when students are asked to "draw the block diagram of an IoT-based system" — it captures the full perception-decision-action pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env1[Physical Environment] --> sen1[Sensor Module]
    sen1 --> sigcon[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
    sigcon --> mcu1[Microcontroller or MCU]
    mcu1 --> cloud1[Cloud Server or Gateway]
    cloud1 --> mcu1
    mcu1 --> drv1[Driver Circuit]
    drv1 --> act1[Actuator Module]
    act1 --> env1
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
1. The **Physical Environment** contains the parameter to be measured (e.g., temperature).
2. The **Sensor** converts it into a weak electrical signal.
3. **Signal Conditioning + ADC** amplifies, filters, and digitizes the signal.
4. The **MCU** processes the data and decides what action to take.
5. The **Cloud** can also send override commands back to the MCU.
6. The **Driver Circuit** (e.g., relay driver, motor driver L293D) boosts the low-power MCU signal.
7. The **Actuator** performs a physical action on the environment, **closing the loop**.

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Sensors and Actuators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1[IoT Physical Layer Devices]
    root1 --> sen_branch[Sensors]
    root1 --> act_branch[Actuators]

    sen_branch --> active_s[Active Sensors]
    sen_branch --> passive_s[Passive Sensors]
    sen_branch --> analog_s[Analog Sensors]
    sen_branch --> digital_s[Digital Sensors]

    active_s --> ex_active1[Photovoltaic Cell]
    active_s --> ex_active2[Piezoelectric Sensor]
    active_s --> ex_active3[Thermocouple]
    passive_s --> ex_passive1[Thermistor]
    passive_s --> ex_passive2[LDR Light Sensor]
    passive_s --> ex_passive3[Strain Gauge]

    act_branch --> elec_a[Electrical Actuators]
    act_branch --> mech_a[Mechanical Actuators]
    act_branch --> hyd_a[Hydraulic Actuators]
    act_branch --> pneu_a[Pneumatic Actuators]
    act_branch --> therm_a[Thermal Actuators]

    elec_a --> ex_elec1[DC Motor]
    elec_a --> ex_elec2[Servo Motor]
    elec_a --> ex_elec3[Relay]
    elec_a --> ex_elec4[Stepper Motor]
    therm_a --> ex_therm1[Heating Element]
    therm_a --> ex_therm2[Peltier Module]
```

## 4.3 Signal Conditioning Subsystem (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw_in[Raw Sensor Output] --> amp1[Amplifier Stage]
    amp1 --> filt1[Filter Stage]
    filt1 --> adc1[ADC Conversion]
    adc1 --> mcu_in[Digital Input to MCU]
```

**Why This Block Matters:** Almost no raw sensor output is directly readable by a microcontroller. A typical LM35 outputs only **10 mV/°C**, so a 30 °C reading gives just 0.3 V — far below the 0–5 V ADC range. An amplifier (e.g., op-amp LM358) boosts this to a usable voltage before digitization.

## 4.4 Smart Home IoT Use Case (End-to-End Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user1[User Smartphone App] --> cloud2[Cloud MQTT Broker]
    cloud2 --> esp1[ESP32 Microcontroller]
    esp1 --> dht_s[DHT11 Temperature Sensor]
    esp1 --> pir_s[PIR Motion Sensor]
    esp1 --> rel_d[Relay Driver Circuit]
    rel_d --> fan_m[Ceiling Fan Motor]
    rel_d --> bulb_l[Smart LED Bulb]
    dht_s --> esp1
    pir_s --> esp1
```

**Reading:** When the **DHT11** detects temperature > 30 °C, the **ESP32** sends a HIGH signal to the **relay driver**, which switches ON the **fan motor**. Simultaneously, when the **PIR sensor** detects motion at night, the **smart bulb** is switched ON. All events are published to the **MQTT broker** for the smartphone app.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **active** and **passive** sensors. Give **two examples** of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | **Active Sensor** | **Passive Sensor** |
|---|---|---|
| External power (excitation) | **Not required** | **Required** |
| Working principle | Self-generates electrical signal | Modulates external signal |
| Energy conversion | Physical → Electrical (direct) | External excitation + modulation |

**Examples of Active Sensors:** (i) **Thermocouple** (Seebeck effect) (ii) **Piezoelectric sensor** (mechanical stress → voltage).

**Examples of Passive Sensors:** (i) **Thermistor** (temperature → resistance change) (ii) **LDR** (light → resistance change).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Q2.** Define the following terms with respect to sensors: (a) **Sensitivity**, (b) **Hysteresis**, (c) **Resolution**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

(a) **Sensitivity (S):** The ratio of the change in sensor output ($\Delta y$) to the change in the input physical quantity ($\Delta x$).
$$
S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} \quad \text{Unit: V/unit of input}
$$

(b) **Hysteresis:** The maximum difference in sensor output for the **same input value** when approached from the **increasing** direction vs. the **decreasing** direction. Expressed as % of full scale.

(c) **Resolution:** The **smallest measurable change** in input that produces a detectable change in output. For digital sensors with n-bit ADC, $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Style)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**(a)** Explain the functional **block diagram of a sensor-actuator closed-loop IoT system**, identifying each block and its role. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A temperature sensor has a sensitivity of **0.02 V/°C** and an offset of **0.5 V** at 0 °C. If the temperature is **75 °C**, calculate: (i) the output voltage, (ii) the sensor's range if the maximum output is 5 V. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

The **closed-loop sensor-actuator IoT system** consists of the following blocks, in order:

1. **Physical Environment (1 mark):** The real-world source of the physical quantity (e.g., room temperature, soil moisture).

2. **Sensor (1 mark):** Converts the physical input into a proportional weak electrical signal. Example: LM35, DHT11.

3. **Signal Conditioning Circuit (1 mark):** Includes an **amplifier** (e.g., op-amp LM358) to amplify weak signals, and a **filter** (e.g., low-pass RC) to remove high-frequency noise.

4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) (1 mark):** Converts the conditioned analog voltage into a digital word that the MCU can read. Resolution $= \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$.

5. **Microcontroller (MCU) (1 mark):** The "brain" of the system. It reads the digitized sensor value, applies thresholds, runs algorithms, and decides the actuator command. Examples: ESP32, Arduino Uno, Raspberry Pi Pico.

6. **Driver / Power Electronics (1 mark):** Boosts the low-current MCU output to drive high-power actuators. Examples: Relay driver (BC547 + 5 V relay), L293D motor driver, MOSFET switch.

7. **Actuator (1 mark):** Converts the electrical control signal into a physical action. Example: DC motor (rotates a fan), solenoid valve (opens water flow), buzzer (alerts).

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Sensitivity $S = 0.02\ \text{V/°C}$, Offset $b = 0.5\ \text{V}$, $T = 75\ \text{°C}$.

**(i) Output Voltage (3 marks):**
$$
V_{out} = S \cdot T + b
$$
$$
V_{out} = (0.02\ \text{V/°C}) \times 75\ \text{°C} + 0.5\ \text{V}
$$
$$
V_{out} = 1.5\ \text{V} + 0.5\ \text{V} = 2.0\ \text{V}
$$

**(ii) Sensor Range (4 marks):**
Maximum output voltage $V_{max} = 5\ \text{V}$.
$$
T_{max} = \frac{V_{max} - b}{S} = \frac{5 - 0.5}{0.02} = \frac{4.5}{0.02} = 225\ \text{°C}
$$
Since the minimum input is 0 °C, the **range is 0 °C to 225 °C**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Describe **five important performance characteristics** of sensors. Briefly explain why each is important in IoT applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of a **thermistor-based temperature sensing circuit**. Derive the **NTC thermistor equation** $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ and calculate the resistance of a thermistor at **100 °C (373 K)**, given $R_0 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 298\ \text{K}$ and material constant $B = 3950\ \text{K}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks (1.4 marks each)

1. **Accuracy (1.4 marks):** Closeness of measured value to true value. Critical in medical IoT (e.g., body temperature sensors) where false readings cause misdiagnosis.

2. **Precision (1.4 marks):** Reproducibility of measurements under identical conditions. Important in industrial IoT (e.g., pressure gauges in pipelines) for process control.

3. **Sensitivity (1.4 marks):** Ability to detect small input changes. High sensitivity is required in gas-leak detectors and biomedical sensors.

4. **Response Time (1.4 marks):** Speed of output to reach 63.2 % of final value. Crucial in **real-time** applications like airbag crash sensors (response time < 10 ms).

5. **Hysteresis (1.4 marks):** Memory effect causing different up/down readings. Must be minimized in **smart thermostats** to avoid oscillation in ON/OFF control.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Operation (3 marks):** A **thermistor** is a temperature-sensitive resistor. In an NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) type, resistance **decreases** as temperature increases. It is connected in a **voltage divider circuit** with a fixed resistor. The voltage at the divider node is fed to the **ADC of a microcontroller**. As temperature changes, the voltage changes, allowing the MCU to compute the temperature using the NTC equation.

**Derivation of NTC Equation (2 marks):**  
The resistance of an NTC thermistor follows an exponential relationship with absolute temperature. Using the B-parameter model:
$$
R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}
$$
This is derived from the Arrhenius-type semiconductor conduction equation and is a standard empirical fit.

**Calculation (2 marks):**  
Given: $R_0 = 10\,000\ \Omega$, $T_0 = 298\ \text{K}$, $T = 373\ \text{K}$, $B = 3950\ \text{K}$.
$$
\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{373} - \frac{1}{298}
$$
$$
= 0.002681 - 0.003356 = -0.000675\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$
$$
B \cdot \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) = 3950 \times (-0.000675) = -2.666
$$
$$
R_T = 10000 \cdot e^{-2.666} = 10000 \times 0.0697 \approx 697\ \Omega
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Unit Mistake in Sensitivity (–2 marks):** Many students write $0.02\ \text{V/°C}$ as $0.02\ \text{V/°F}$ or omit the unit entirely. Always include units in the final answer.
> - **Confusing Sensor with Transducer (–1 mark):** All sensors are transducers, but **not all transducers are sensors**. Sensors only convert *non-electrical → electrical*. A speaker is a transducer but **not** a sensor.
> - **Active vs. Passive Confusion (–2 marks):** A **thermocouple** is ACTIVE (no excitation needed), while a **thermistor** is PASSIVE (needs excitation). Mixing these up is a classic error.
> - **Missing "Active Digital Output" Distinction (–1 mark):** HC-SR04 has both a *trigger pulse* input and an *echo pulse* output — many students forget to mention the *time-of-flight* calculation.
> - **Numerical: Forgetting to Convert °C to K (–2 marks):** Thermistor equations **always** require **absolute temperature in Kelvin**. Substituting 100 °C instead of 373 K will yield a wildly wrong answer.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Sensors and Actuators (KTU 2024):**

- **Sensor** = device that converts a **physical quantity → electrical signal**. It is the *perception* unit of an IoT system.
- **Actuator** = device that converts an **electrical signal → physical action**. It is the *action* unit of an IoT system.
- **Active sensors** generate their own signal (e.g., thermocouple, piezoelectric, solar cell). **Passive sensors** require external excitation (e.g., thermistor, LDR, strain gauge).
- **Analog sensors** produce a continuous voltage/current output (e.g., LM35, MQ-2). **Digital sensors** produce a digital word or pulse (e.g., DHT11, DS18B20, MPU6050).
- The **sensitivity** of a sensor is the slope of its input-output curve: $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$.
- **% Error** is computed as $\left\vert \dfrac{V_m - V_t}{V_t} \right\vert \times 100\%$.
- A **first-order sensor's step response** follows $y(t) = y_{\infty} (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$, where $\tau$ is the time-constant and $y(\tau) = 0.632\,y_{\infty}$.
- **Hysteresis** is the difference in output when the same input is approached from above vs. below; it is a key source of error.
- **Resolution** for an n-bit ADC = $\dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$.
- **RTD** equation: $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ (linear, used for industrial platinum PT100 sensors).
- **Thermistor** equation: $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ — requires **absolute temperature (Kelvin)**.
- **Thermocouple** follows the **Seebeck effect**: $V = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$, where $\alpha$ is the Seebeck coefficient.
- **Actuators** are classified as electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, thermal, mechanical, optical, and chemical. In KTU IoT labs, you will mostly use **electrical actuators** (DC motor, servo, relay, buzzer, LED).
- A **signal conditioning circuit** is almost always required between the sensor and the MCU (amplification + filtering + ADC).
- A **driver circuit** (relay, L293D, MOSFET) is required between the MCU and a high-power actuator.
- Common IoT sensors: **DHT11, DS18B20, LM35, HC-SR04, MQ-135, PIR, MPU6050, LDR, BMP180, Soil Moisture**.
- Common IoT actuators: **DC motor, servo (SG90), stepper, relay, solenoid, buzzer, LED, Peltier, heating element**.
- The **closed-loop IoT architecture** is: *Environment → Sensor → Signal Conditioning → ADC → MCU → Driver → Actuator → Environment*.
- In KTU Part-B answers, **always draw a labeled block diagram** — it typically earns 1–2 easy marks even if the rest is incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 IoT Hardware – Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Internet of Things (PEEET634)*, **IoT Hardware** refers to the complete physical layer of an IoT system — the tangible electronic components, embedded boards, sensing elements, actuation mechanisms, and communication modules that collectively enable an object to **sense, compute, communicate, and actuate** within a connected ecosystem.

According to the layered IoT Reference Architecture, hardware constitutes the **Perception Layer (Sensors & Actuators)** and the **Network/Edge Layer (Gateways, Microcontrollers, Single-Board Computers)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2 Focus):** IoT Hardware encompasses Sensors, Actuators, Embedded Microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino), Single-Board Computers (e.g., Raspberry Pi), and Communication Modules (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa) — the *physical embodiment* of the IoT stack.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Human Body" View

Imagine an IoT system as a **living human body**:

| IoT Component | Human Body Analogy | Function |
|---------------|--------------------|----------|
| **Sensors** | Eyes, Ears, Skin (Nervous System) | Perceive the environment (light, temperature, motion) |
| **Actuators** | Muscles, Hands | Perform physical actions (rotate motor, open valve) |
| **Microcontroller (MCU)** | Brain Stem (Spinal Cord) | Low-level, real-time reflexive decisions |
| **Single-Board Computer (SBC)** | Cerebrum (Thinking Brain) | High-level computation, OS-driven processing |
| **Communication Module** | Mouth & Nerves | Speak/Listen to other devices (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth) |
| **Power Supply** | Heart (circulatory system) | Distributes energy to all organs |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a human cannot function without eyes, a brain, and muscles, an IoT system is incomplete without sensors (input), a controller (decision), and actuators (output). The **microcontroller** is the *digital reflex* — fast and deterministic, while the **SBC** is the *digital thinker* — capable of running an OS and complex logic.

## 1.3 Key Hardware Categories at a Glance

The KTU 2024 Scheme groups IoT hardware into **four** major functional classes:

1. **Sensing Subsystem** – Converts physical phenomena → electrical signals.
2. **Processing Subsystem** – Microcontrollers (Arduino Uno, NodeMCU/ESP8266, ESP32, STM32) and SBCs (Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone).
3. **Actuation Subsystem** – Motors, Relays, Servos, Solenoids, LED arrays.
4. **Communication Subsystem** – Transceivers: Wi-Fi (ESP8266), BLE, LoRa, ZigBee, NFC, RFID.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** Most IoT sensors output either **analog voltage (0–5 V or 0–3.3 V)** or **digital protocols (I²C, SPI, UART, OneWire)**. A **16-bit ADC** has a resolution of $1/65536 \approx 0.0153\%$ per Least Significant Bit (LSB).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensors – The Perception Layer

A **sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, light) into a measurable electrical signal. IoT sensors are characterized by the following engineering parameters:

### 2.1.1 Static Characteristics of Sensors

- **Accuracy:** Closeness of reading to the *true* value. Expressed as $\pm$% of Full-Scale Output (FSO).
- **Precision:** Repeatability of measurement under identical conditions.
- **Sensitivity:** Ratio of output change $\Delta y$ to input change $\Delta x$.
- **Resolution:** Smallest detectable increment (related to ADC bit-depth).
- **Range (Span):** Min and Max measurable input.
- **Linearity:** How closely the output follows a straight-line transfer function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Static Transfer Function** of an ideal linear sensor:
> $$y = mx + c$$
> where $m$ is **sensitivity** and $c$ is the **offset/bias**.

### 2.1.2 Dynamic Characteristics

- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time to go from **10% to 90%** of final value.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time to remain within $\pm 2\%$ of steady-state value.
- **Bandwidth:** Frequency range where amplitude response is within $-3$ dB.
- **Hysteresis:** Difference in output for the same input approached from opposite directions.

### 2.1.3 Classification of IoT Sensors

| Class | Examples | Output | Typical IoT Use |
|-------|----------|--------|-----------------|
| Temperature | DHT11, DHT22, LM35, DS18B20, BME280 | Analog / Digital | HVAC, Cold-chain |
| Humidity | DHT22, BME280, SHT31 | Digital (I²C) | Greenhouses |
| Light | LDR, BH1750, TSL2561 | Analog / I²C | Smart streetlights |
| Motion / PIR | HC-SR501, ADXL345 | Digital / I²C/SPI | Security, wearables |
| Gas / Chemical | MQ-2, MQ-135, BME680 | Analog | Air-quality monitors |
| Proximity / Distance | HC-SR04, VL53L0X | PWM / I²C | Parking, robotics |
| Pressure | BMP280, MPX5700 | I²C / Analog | Altitude, weather |
| Image / Vision | OV2640, Pi Camera | Parallel / CSI | Surveillance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Passive vs Active Sensors:** A *passive* sensor (e.g., LDR, thermistor) requires no external power; it generates a signal from the measured energy. An *active* sensor (e.g., ultrasonic HC-SR04) emits energy and measures the response — it needs excitation.

## 2.2 Actuators – The Action Layer

An **actuator** is the inverse transducer: it converts electrical signals → physical action. In KTU Module 2, the most frequently tested actuators are:

- **DC Motors** – Continuous rotation, controlled via H-bridge (L298N) or PWM.
- **Servo Motors** – Precise angular position (0°–180°) using PWM pulses.
- **Stepper Motors** – Discrete step rotation; used in 3D printers, CNC.
- **Relays** – Electromechanical switches; rated in **Amperes** at **Volts** (e.g., 10A @ 250VAC).
- **Solenoids** – Linear push/pull motion (e.g., door locks, valves).
- **Piezo Buzzers** – Audible signaling (e.g., alarms).

### Servo Motor Control Formula

A standard hobby servo is controlled by a **50 Hz PWM** signal (period $T = 20$ ms). The pulse width $W$ determines the shaft angle $\theta$:

$$\theta = \frac{W - 1.0\text{ ms}}{1.0\text{ ms}} \times 90° \quad \text{for } 0° \le \theta \le 180°$$

- $W = 1.0$ ms → $0°$
- $W = 1.5$ ms → $90°$
- $W = 2.0$ ms → $180°$

## 2.3 Microcontrollers vs. Single-Board Computers

This is a **high-yield KTU question** — the distinction is often asked for 3 to 14 marks.

| Parameter | Microcontroller (MCU) | Single-Board Computer (SBC) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Examples | Arduino Uno, ESP32, STM32, NodeMCU | Raspberry Pi 4/5, BeagleBone Black |
| CPU | 8/32-bit, low clock (16–240 MHz) | 64-bit, multi-core (1.5 GHz+) |
| OS | Bare-metal / RTOS (no full OS) | Linux (Raspbian, Ubuntu) |
| RAM | 2 KB – 512 KB | 1 GB – 8 GB |
| Power | < 0.5 W (battery friendly) | 3–7 W |
| Real-time | **Hard real-time** deterministic | Soft real-time, latency jitter |
| Cost | ₹200 – ₹800 | ₹3,000 – ₹8,000 |
| Ideal For | Sensing, control loops, wearables | Edge AI, gateway, vision processing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** If your IoT node *only* needs to read a sensor every few seconds and toggle a relay, use a **Microcontroller (ESP32)**. If it needs to run a database, host a web server, or process images, use a **Raspberry Pi**.

## 2.4 The Arduino Uno – The KTU Favorite

The **Arduino Uno R3** is based on the **ATmega328P** 8-bit AVR MCU. Its key specs are examinable:

| Spec | Value |
|------|-------|
| MCU | ATmega328P |
| Clock | **16 MHz** |
| Flash Memory | **32 KB** (0.5 KB bootloader) |
| SRAM | 2 KB |
| EEPROM | 1 KB |
| Digital I/O | 14 (6 PWM) |
| Analog Inputs | 6 (10-bit ADC) |
| Operating Voltage | 5 V |
| Input Voltage | 7–12 V (recommended) |

### 10-bit ADC Conversion

The Arduino Uno's ADC maps $0$ V → $0$ and $5$ V → $1023$:

$$V_{in} = \frac{ADC_{reading}}{1023} \times V_{ref}$$

If $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V and $ADC_{reading} = 512$:

$$V_{in} = \frac{512}{1023} \times 5.0 \approx 2.502 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution per LSB** = $V_{ref} / 2^n = 5.0 / 1024 = 4.883$ mV.

## 2.5 NodeMCU & ESP32 – Wi-Fi Enabled MCUs

- **NodeMCU (ESP8266):** 32-bit Tensilica L106, 80 MHz, 4 MB flash, built-in **Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n**, **$1$ ADC** (10-bit, 0–1 V), GPIO limited.
- **ESP32:** Dual-core Tensilica Xtensa LX6, 240 MHz, **Wi-Fi + Bluetooth (BLE 4.2)**, **18 ADC channels (12-bit)**, hardware crypto, ultra-low-power coprocessor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Warning:** ESP8266/ESP32 GPIOs are **3.3 V tolerant only** — connecting 5 V will permanently damage the chip. Use a **level shifter** when interfacing with 5 V sensors (e.g., HC-SR04).

## 2.6 Communication Hardware Modules

| Module | Frequency | Range | Power | IoT Use Case |
|--------|-----------|-------|-------|--------------|
| Wi-Fi (ESP32) | 2.4 GHz | 50–100 m | High | Home automation |
| BLE (HM-10) | 2.4 GHz | 10 m | Very low | Wearables, beacons |
| LoRa (SX1276) | 433/868/915 MHz | **2–15 km** | Very low | Agriculture, smart city |
| ZigBee (XBee S2C) | 2.4 GHz | 30–100 m | Low | Mesh sensor networks |
| NFC/RFID (RC522) | 13.56 MHz | < 10 cm | Passive | Access control |
| GSM (SIM800) | 850/900/1800/1900 MHz | Cell-tower range | Medium | Vehicle tracking |

## 2.7 Power Subsystem

IoT nodes are often battery-powered. Power considerations:

- **Sleep Current:** ESP32 deep-sleep = **10 µA**; Arduino idle = ~15 mA.
- **Battery Sizing (Peukert-adjusted):**
$$T_{life} = \frac{C \times V_{batt}}{P_{avg}}$$

  where $C$ is capacity in **Ah**, $V_{batt}$ is nominal voltage, $P_{avg}$ is average power in **W**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Harvesting (advanced):** Solar, piezoelectric, and thermal harvesters can extend node lifetime indefinitely in outdoor deployments.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---------|---------|--------------|
| Sensor Sensitivity | $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$ | Output per unit input |
| ADC Conversion | $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC}{2^n - 1} \times V_{ref}$ | $n$ = bit-depth |
| ADC Resolution (LSB) | $\Delta V = V_{ref}/2^n$ | Volts per step |
| Servo Angle | $\theta = \dfrac{W - 1.0\text{ ms}}{1.0\text{ ms}} \times 90°$ | For $0°$–$180°$ |
| Battery Life | $T = \dfrac{C \times V}{P_{avg}}$ | Hours |
| Frequency from Period | $f = 1/T$ | Hz |
| PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{off}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| Power | $P = V \times I$ | Watts |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC}{2^n}$ instead of $2^n - 1$. Remember: $n$-bit ADC has $2^n$ *steps* but only $2^n - 1$ *intervals*. The maximum count is $2^n - 1$ (e.g., 1023, not 1024).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Resolution and Quantization Error

We will derive the **resolution (LSB size)** and the **maximum quantization error** for a generic $n$-bit ADC used in IoT microcontrollers.

### Step 1 — Define the ADC Full-Scale Range

The full-scale analog input range is the difference between the reference voltage $V_{ref}^+$ and the negative reference $V_{ref}^-$:

$$V_{FSR} = V_{ref}^+ - V_{ref}^-$$

For Arduino Uno and ESP32 (single-ended), $V_{ref}^- = 0$ V and $V_{ref}^+ = V_{ref}$, so:

$$V_{FSR} = V_{ref}$$

### Step 2 — Count the Discrete Digital Codes

An $n$-bit ADC produces integer codes from $0$ to $2^n - 1$. The total *number* of codes is:

$$N_{codes} = 2^n - 1$$

For $n = 10$ (Arduino): $N_{codes} = 1023$.

### Step 3 — Derive the Resolution (LSB Size)

The LSB size $\Delta V$ is the voltage represented by **one** step:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

For Arduino Uno ($n=10$, $V_{ref}=5$ V):

$$\Delta V = \frac{5.0}{1023} \approx 4.886 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 4.886 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 4 — Quantization Error

The **maximum quantization error** is half the LSB size, because a real analog value can fall anywhere within a code's "bucket":

$$E_{q, max} = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2 \cdot (2^n - 1)}$$

For Arduino Uno:

$$E_{q, max} = \pm \frac{5.0}{2 \times 1023} \approx \pm 2.443 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 5 — Recovering Voltage from ADC Reading

Given a digital code $D$ read from the ADC:

$$V_{in} = D \cdot \Delta V = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

This matches the formula already presented in the cheat sheet and is the most-tested equation in KTU ESE Module 2 questions.

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An ESP32 (12-bit ADC, $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V) returns a digital value of $D = 2500$. What is the input voltage?

**Solution:**

$$V_{in} = \frac{2500 \times 3.3}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{2500 \times 3.3}{4095}$$

$$V_{in} = \frac{8250}{4095} \approx 2.015 \text{ V}$$

The LSB size is:

$$\Delta V = \frac{3.3}{4095} \approx 0.806 \text{ mV per step}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Battery Lifetime for an IoT Sensor Node

### Step 1 — Define Energy Budget

A battery of capacity $C$ in **mAh** at nominal voltage $V_{batt}$ stores total energy:

$$E_{total} = C \times V_{batt} \quad \text{[mWh]}$$

### Step 2 — Average Power Consumption

If the node operates in duty-cycled mode — active for time $t_{on}$ at current $I_{on}$, then sleeping for $t_{sleep}$ at $I_{sleep}$ — the average current is:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{I_{on} \cdot t_{on} + I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}}{t_{on} + t_{sleep}}$$

### Step 3 — Battery Lifetime in Hours

$$T_{life} \text{ [hours]} = \frac{C \text{ [mAh]}}{I_{avg} \text{ [mA]}}$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

**Problem:** An ESP32 node wakes every 60 s, transmits for 3 s at 160 mA, then sleeps at 10 µA. Battery: 2000 mAh @ 3.7 V. Find lifetime.

**Solution:**

$$I_{avg} = \frac{(160 \text{ mA})(3 \text{ s}) + (0.01 \text{ mA})(57 \text{ s})}{60 \text{ s}}$$

$$I_{avg} = \frac{480 + 0.57}{60} = \frac{480.57}{60} \approx 8.01 \text{ mA}$$

$$T_{life} = \frac{2000 \text{ mAh}}{8.01 \text{ mA}} \approx 249.7 \text{ hours} \approx 10.4 \text{ days}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The dominant energy consumer is the *active* phase — reducing transmission duration has a larger impact than reducing sleep current. This is why protocols like **LoRaWAN** use class-A devices that sleep nearly 99% of the time.

## 3.3 Derivation: Servo Angle from PWM Pulse Width

### Step 1 — Define the Mapping Function

A hobby servo accepts PWM at $f = 50$ Hz, i.e., period $T = 20$ ms. The pulse width $W$ maps linearly to angle $\theta \in [0°, 180°]$:

- $W = 1.0$ ms ↔ $\theta = 0°$
- $W = 2.0$ ms ↔ $\theta = 180°$

### Step 2 — Linear Interpolation

$$\frac{\theta - 0°}{180° - 0°} = \frac{W - 1.0}{2.0 - 1.0}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\theta$

$$\theta = \frac{W - 1.0}{1.0} \times 90°$$

Equivalently, in terms of duty cycle $D = W/T$:

$$\theta = (0.05 + 0.05 D) \times 1800 \quad \text{(for } D \text{ from 5% to 10%)}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example

**Problem:** Arduino `analogWrite` produces pulse width $W = 1.5$ ms on a 50 Hz PWM. Find the servo angle.

**Solution:**

$$\theta = \frac{1.5 - 1.0}{1.0} \times 90° = 0.5 \times 90° = 45°$$

So $W = 1.5$ ms corresponds to the **neutral** (90°) position — wait, recalculating:

$$\theta = (W - 1.0) \times 90° = (1.5 - 1.0) \times 90° = 45°$$

Correction: The standard convention is:

$$\theta = (W - 1.0) \times 180° \quad \text{over range } 1.0 \le W \le 2.0 \text{ ms}$$

Hence $W = 1.5$ ms gives $\theta = 90°$ (center). The earlier formula is dimensionally inconsistent; the correct canonical form is:

$$\theta = \frac{(W - 1.0 \text{ ms}) \times 180°}{1.0 \text{ ms}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** Always check the units in PWM-to-angle formulas. Many textbooks write $W$ in milliseconds and divide by 1 ms — students often forget the unit normalization and lose marks.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hardware Decision

| Need | Recommended Hardware | Justification |
|------|----------------------|---------------|
| Soil moisture in a remote farm | ESP32 + LoRa + Solar panel | Long-range, low-power, no Wi-Fi |
| Smart doorbell with video | Raspberry Pi 4 + Pi Camera + Wi-Fi | Needs image processing & streaming |
| Wearable heart-rate monitor | nRF52840 (BLE MCU) | Ultra-low-power BLE, body-area |
| Industrial vibration monitor | STM32 + Accelerometer | Hard real-time, deterministic sampling |
| Pet tracker (indoor) | ESP32 + BLE beacon | Cheap, sufficient range |
| Air-quality dashboard | Arduino + MQ-135 + ESP-01 | Simple, no OS, cheap Wi-Fi add-on |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic IoT Node Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PW[Power Supply Battery or USB] --> REG[Voltage Regulator 3.3V 5V]
    REG --> MCU[Microcontroller Arduino or ESP32]
    SENS1[Sensor 1 Temperature] --> MCU
    SENS2[Sensor 2 Humidity] --> MCU
    SENS3[Sensor 3 Motion] --> MCU
    ACT1[Actuator Relay] --> MCU
    ACT2[Actuator Motor] --> MCU
    COMM[Communication Module Wi-Fi BLE LoRa] --> MCU
    MCU --> CLOUD[(Cloud Server)]
    CLOUD --> USER[User Mobile App or Dashboard]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram is the **canonical answer** for the question *"Draw and explain the architecture of an IoT node."* It is worth up to 7 marks in KTU ESE.

## 4.2 Sensor Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[IoT Sensors] --> A[Active Sensors Need Excitation]

    A --> A2[Radar Doppler]
    A --> A3[Infrared Proximity]
    P --> P1[Thermal LM35 DHT22]
    P --> P2[Light LDR Photodiode]
    P --> P3[Chemical MQ-2 Gas Sensor]
    P --> P4[Mechanical Piezo Strain Gauge]
```

## 4.3 Communication Module Spectrum (Range vs Power)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SHORT[Short Range High Power]

        BLE[BLE 2.4 GHz Range 10 m]
    end

        WIFI[Wi-Fi 2.4 GHz Range 100 m]
        ZB[ZigBee 2.4 GHz Range 100 m]
    end
```

## 4.4 Arduino–Sensor–Actuator Wiring Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AOUT[Analog Output from LM35] --> A0[Arduino Pin A0]
    DHT[DHT22 Data Pin] --> D2[Arduino Pin D2]
    TRIG[HC-SR04 Trigger] --> D8[Arduino Pin D8]
    ECHO[HC-SR04 Echo] --> D9[Arduino Pin D9]
    SERVO[Servo Signal] --> D10[Arduino Pin D10 PWM]
    RELAY[Relay IN] --> D7[Arduino Pin D7]
    A0 --> MCU[ATmega328P]
    D2 --> MCU
    D8 --> MCU
    D9 --> MCU
    D10 --> MCU
    D7 --> MCU
    MCU --> USB[USB to PC for Serial Monitor]
```

## 4.5 Power Distribution Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Source Battery 7.4 V or USB 5 V] --> SW[Slide Switch]
    SW --> REG1[LM7805 5 V Regulator]
    SW --> REG2[AMS1117 3.3 V LDO]
    REG1 --> ARD[Arduino 5 V Rail]
    REG2 --> ESP[ESP32 3.3 V Rail]
    REG2 --> SENS[3.3 V Sensors]
    ARD --> ACT[5 V Relays and Servos]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two regulators?** The Arduino Uno's onboard regulator is inefficient and noisy. For a clean, regulated supply to sensitive Wi-Fi MCUs like the ESP32, a dedicated **AMS1117-3.3** LDO is preferred.

## 4.6 Hardware Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Needs Wi-Fi or Internet?} -->|Yes| Q2{Runs Heavy Compute or Vision?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Needs Long Range 1 km plus?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| PI[Raspberry Pi SBC]
    Q2 -->|No| ESP[ESP32 or NodeMCU MCU]
    Q3 -->|Yes| LR[LoRa Module plus Arduino or ESP32]
    Q3 -->|No| ARD[Arduino Uno or Nano]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Microcontroller and a Single-Board Computer with two suitable examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

A *Microcontroller (MCU)* is a single-chip computer containing CPU, RAM, ROM, and peripherals, designed for real-time control tasks. It typically runs bare-metal firmware or a simple RTOS, with kilobytes of RAM and low clock speeds. **Examples:** ATmega328P (Arduino Uno), ESP32.

A *Single-Board Computer (SBC)* is a full computer built on a single PCB, with a multi-core processor, GBs of RAM, capable of running a complete Operating System (Linux). **Examples:** Raspberry Pi 4, BeagleBone Black.

| Parameter | MCU | SBC |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| OS | None / RTOS | Full Linux |
| RAM | KB | GB |
| Use | Sensing & Control | Gateway & AI |

*(2 marks for differences + 1 mark for examples — total 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any three static characteristics of a sensor and define each in one line.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Accuracy** – Closeness of the measured value to the true value. *(1 mark)*
2. **Sensitivity** – Ratio of change in output to change in input. *(1 mark)*
3. **Linearity** – Degree to which the output is directly proportional to input. *(1 mark)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an IoT node with a neat block diagram. List the functions of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of an Ultrasonic Distance Sensor (HC-SR04). Derive the formula for distance measurement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The architecture of an IoT node consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Sensing Subsystem** – Sensors like DHT22, MQ-135, and LDR convert physical quantities to electrical signals.
2. **Processing Subsystem** – A microcontroller (e.g., ESP32) or SBC (Raspberry Pi) processes the data, runs decision logic.
3. **Actuation Subsystem** – Relays, motors, and LEDs perform actions based on commands.
4. **Communication Subsystem** – Wi-Fi, BLE, or LoRa modules transmit data to the cloud/gateway.
5. **Power Subsystem** – Battery + voltage regulators (3.3 V, 5 V) power all blocks.
6. **User Interface (optional)** – LCD / OLED / Mobile app for local feedback.

*(Block diagram in Section 4.1 may be redrawn here for 2 marks; 3 marks for listing; 2 marks for function explanation.)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

The **HC-SR04** ultrasonic sensor measures distance by emitting a 40 kHz ultrasonic pulse and listening for its echo.

**Working Principle:**

- The trigger pin receives a **10 µs HIGH pulse** from the MCU.
- The sensor emits 8 cycles of 40 kHz burst.
- The sound wave travels to the target, reflects, and returns.
- The Echo pin goes HIGH for a duration $T$ proportional to the distance.

**Derivation of Distance:**

Let $v$ be the speed of sound in air (≈ 343 m/s at 20 °C). The wave travels to the target and back, covering $2d$ in time $T$:

$$2d = v \cdot T \implies d = \frac{v \cdot T}{2}$$

**Numerical Example:** If $T = 588$ µs, then:

$$d = \frac{343 \times 588 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.2017}{2} \approx 0.1008 \text{ m} \approx 10 \text{ cm}$$

*(2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for working, 2 marks for derivation — total 7 marks.)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Compare Arduino Uno and Raspberry Pi 4 on the basis of architecture, power, OS, and at least three other parameters. When would you prefer a Raspberry Pi over an Arduino? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how an **MQ-2 gas sensor** interfaces with an ESP32. Draw the circuit and write the formula for converting analog reading to gas concentration in ppm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Parameter | Arduino Uno | Raspberry Pi 4 |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| Architecture | 8-bit AVR (ATmega328P) | 64-bit ARM Cortex-A72 quad-core |
| Clock | 16 MHz | 1.5 GHz |
| RAM | 2 KB SRAM | 1–8 GB LPDDR4 |
| Flash / Storage | 32 KB Flash | microSD (GB) |
| OS | None (bare-metal) | Linux (Raspbian) |
| Power | ~0.5 W | ~3–7 W |
| Real-time | Hard real-time | Soft real-time |
| GPIO | 14 digital + 6 analog | 40 GPIO (no native ADC) |
| Cost | ~₹400 | ~₹5,000 |

**When to prefer Raspberry Pi over Arduino:**
- When the application requires **image processing, video streaming, hosting a web server, or running a database**.
- Example: A smart doorbell with face-recognition uses Pi (vision processing) + Arduino (door lock control).
- Example: An IoT gateway aggregating data from 50 sensor nodes needs a Pi (Linux + MQTT broker).

*(5 marks for table + 2 marks for justification.)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

The **MQ-2** is a metal-oxide semiconductor (MOS) gas sensor sensitive to LPG, methane, propane, and smoke.

**Circuit:**
- $V_{CC}$ (5 V) → H pin of MQ-2
- GND → both GND pins
- Analog output (AO) → ESP32 GPIO 34 (ADC1_CH6)
- Digital output (DO) → ESP32 GPIO 27 (threshold via potentiometer)

**Working:** The sensing layer (SnO₂) changes resistance in the presence of gas. The internal heater vaporizes contaminants, stabilizing the reading.

**PPM Conversion Formula:**

MQ sensors output an analog voltage $V_{out}$ that corresponds to a resistance ratio:

$$\frac{R_s}{R_o} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{out}}{V_{out}}$$

where $R_o$ is the sensor resistance in clean air (calibration constant) and $R_s$ is the variable resistance in gas.

The gas concentration in ppm is computed via the **power-law curve-fit** from the datasheet:

$$\text{PPM} = A \cdot \left(\frac{R_s}{R_o}\right)^{-B}$$

where $A$ and $B$ are gas-specific constants (e.g., for LPG: $A = 1000$, $B = 2.0$).

**Numerical Example:** If $V_{out} = 1.2$ V with $V_{CC} = 5$ V:

$$\frac{R_s}{R_o} = \frac{5 - 1.2}{1.2} = \frac{3.8}{1.2} \approx 3.17$$

$$\text{PPM}_{LPG} = 1000 \times (3.17)^{-2.0} \approx 1000 \times 0.0995 \approx 99.5 \text{ ppm}$$

*(2 marks for circuit, 3 marks for explanation, 2 marks for formula — total 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in IoT Hardware Questions — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in formulas** — e.g., writing $d = v \times T$ instead of $d = (v \times T)/2$ for ultrasonic sensors. The factor of 2 (round-trip) is a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing ESP32's ADC range (0–3.3 V) with Arduino's (0–5 V).** Always state $V_{ref}$ explicitly in numerical answers.
> 3. **Drawing block diagrams without labels** — every block must have a name and a one-line function. Unlabeled boxes fetch zero marks.
> 4. **Mixing up active and passive sensors** — HC-SR04 is **active** (emits ultrasonic pulse); LDR is **passive**.
> 5. **Writing "Arduino = Raspberry Pi"** — these are fundamentally different; comparisons must be in a table with at least **4 parameters**.
> 6. **PPM formula sign error** — $R_s/R_o$ is *inversely* related to gas concentration; using a positive exponent loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IoT Hardware Layers:** Perception (Sensors/Actuators), Edge (MCU/SBC), Network (Communication modules), Application.
- **Sensor Static Parameters:** Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Resolution, Range, Linearity, Hysteresis.
- **Active vs Passive Sensors:** Active = needs external excitation; Passive = self-generating (e.g., LDR).
- **Arduino Uno Key Specs:** ATmega328P, 16 MHz, 32 KB Flash, 2 KB SRAM, 10-bit ADC (5 V reference), 6 analog + 14 digital pins.
- **ESP32 vs ESP8266:** ESP32 is dual-core, has BLE, 18 ADC channels, 12-bit; ESP8266 has 1 ADC, 10-bit, 1 V max.
- **Raspberry Pi:** Linux-capable SBC, **no native ADC** — needs an external ADS1115 for analog sensing.
- **ADC Formula:** $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC}{2^n - 1} \times V_{ref}$ — never use $2^n$ in the denominator.
- **Ultrasonic Distance:** $d = (v \times T)/2$ where $v = 343$ m/s, $T$ is the echo pulse width in seconds.
- **Servo Control:** $f = 50$ Hz; $W \in [1.0, 2.0]$ ms maps linearly to $\theta \in [0°, 180°]$.
- **LoRa Range:** 2–15 km with low power; ideal for agricultural and remote IoT.
- **BLE Range:** ~10 m, ultra-low power, used in wearables.
- **Voltage Level Warning:** ESP32/ESP8266 = **3.3 V only** — never connect 5 V to GPIO.
- **MQ Sensors:** Output resistance varies with gas; convert using $\text{PPM} = A \cdot (R_s/R_o)^{-B}$ with calibration.
- **Battery Lifetime:** $T_{life} = C/I_{avg}$; reduce active-time current, not sleep current, for biggest gains.
- **Communication Choice:** Short range + high data → Wi-Fi; Long range + low data → LoRa; Body-area → BLE; Identification → RFID/NFC.
- **Real-time:** MCUs are hard real-time; SBCs running Linux are soft real-time — choose MCU for control loops.
- **Diagram Rule:** Always label every block; mention one example component per block.
- **Formula Rule:** Always quote the unit and the operating condition (e.g., "at $T = 20$ °C, $v = 343$ m/s").

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SPACES":** **S**ensors, **P**rocessing (MCU/SBC), **A**ctuators, **C**ommunication, **E**nergy (power), **S**ecurity. If your answer covers all five (excluding security for Module 2 hardware focus), you have scored at least 12/14.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Software - Overview of Operating Systems, Firmware, Middleware, Application Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET634 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT Software - Overview of Operating Systems, Firmware, Middleware, Application Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The IoT Software Stack — A Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *IoT Software refers to the layered collection of programs, instruction sets, and platform services that enable IoT devices to acquire data, process it locally, communicate over networks, and deliver user-facing functionality. It spans four critical layers — **Operating Systems (OS)**, **Firmware**, **Middleware**, and **Application Software**.*

The IoT software stack is the *software counterpart* of the IoT hardware architecture. Just as an IoT device has sensors, microcontrollers, and communication modules (hardware), it also requires a software hierarchy to make these components "think, talk, and respond" intelligently.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Home Refrigerator

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Imagine a modern smart refrigerator as a metaphor for an IoT device.

| Software Layer | Smart Fridge Analogy | Engineering Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Firmware** | The factory-programmed instructions burned into the fridge's temperature sensor chip | Low-level hardware control (bit-banging GPIO, ADC reads) |
| **Operating System** | The fridge's internal task scheduler — runs the cooling cycle, defrost timer, display refresh simultaneously | Multitasking, memory mgmt, scheduling on MCU |
| **Middleware** | The "translator" that converts the fridge's internal status (e.g., "compressor ON") into a JSON message that your phone app understands | Service abstraction, data normalization, protocol bridging |
| **Application Software** | The phone app showing you the fridge temperature, letting you change modes, and alerting you when milk is low | User-facing functionality, analytics, dashboards |

So when you check your fridge's temperature from your office, **four distinct software layers** collaborate — this is the essence of the IoT software stack.

## 1.3 Layer-by-Layer Definitions

### 1.3.1 IoT Operating System (IoT OS)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **IoT Operating System** is a specialized, resource-constrained OS designed to run on low-power microcontrollers (8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit MCUs) with limited RAM (often **< 256 KB**) and flash (**< 1 MB**). Unlike general-purpose OSes (Windows, Linux), IoT OSes are optimized for **determinism, low memory footprint, and real-time task scheduling**.

**Examples:** Contiki, RIOT, FreeRTOS, TinyOS, Zephyr OS, Mbed OS, Apache Mynewt, Huawei LiteOS.

### 1.3.2 IoT Firmware
> [!NOTE]
> **Firmware** is the *lowest-level software* permanently programmed into the read-only memory (ROM/Flash) of an IoT device. It directly interfaces with the hardware — toggling GPIO pins, reading sensor registers, driving actuators — without an operating system's abstraction.

Firmware is what makes the bare metal "come alive." It is typically written in **C/C++** and resides closest to the silicon.

### 1.3.3 IoT Middleware
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Middleware** is a *software abstraction layer* sitting between the OS/firmware and the application software. It provides reusable services such as **device management, data filtering, protocol translation (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP), security, context-awareness, and storage** so that developers do not rewrite these services for every application.

It is the "glue" that makes heterogeneous IoT systems interoperable.

### 1.3.4 IoT Application Software
> [!NOTE]
> **Application Software** is the *topmost, user-facing layer* that delivers business logic and end-user functionality — dashboards, mobile apps, alert systems, analytics engines, machine learning inference, and cloud-side processing.

This is what the user directly interacts with (e.g., a mobile app showing your IoT doorbell camera feed).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the IoT Software Stack

### 2.1.1 Operating Systems for IoT — Deep Dive

IoT OSes are categorized into three broad classes:

**1. Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)**
- Designed for **deterministic response** within strict timing deadlines.
- Use **preemptive priority-based scheduling**.
- Memory footprint: typically **< 10 KB** kernel.
- Examples: **FreeRTOS**, **Zephyr OS**, **VxWorks**, **QNX**.

**2. Event-Driven Operating Systems**
- Use a **protothread-based** or **event-handler** model instead of traditional threads.
- Highly memory-efficient; ideal for **wireless sensor networks (WSN)**.
- Examples: **Contiki OS**, **TinyOS**.

**3. Full-Featured OS (Linux-based)**
- Run on more powerful IoT edge gateways (e.g., Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson).
- Memory footprint: **> 32 MB**.
- Examples: **Embedded Linux (Buildroot, Yocto)**, **Windows IoT Core**, **Raspbian**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Why IoT cannot use Windows/Linux directly on MCUs:**
> A standard Linux kernel requires $\geq 1$ MB of RAM and a **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**. Most IoT MCUs (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0/M3) lack an MMU and have $\leq 256$ KB SRAM. Hence a *specialized* IoT OS is mandatory.

### 2.1.2 Firmware — Deep Dive

Firmware operates at **three privilege levels** within an MCU:

| Privilege Level | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Bootloader** | Initial code that initializes clocks, RAM, and loads main firmware | STM32 System Memory Bootloader |
| **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** | Vendor-supplied code to access peripherals | STM32 HAL, ESP-IDF Drivers |
| **Application Firmware** | User-written logic for the IoT use case | Custom temperature logger code |

**Key Firmware Properties:**
- **Non-volatile residence:** Stored in Flash/ROM/EEPROM.
- **Update mechanism:** Modern IoT firmware supports **OTA (Over-the-Air) updates**.
- **Boot modes:** Dual-bank A/B partitioning enables **fail-safe firmware rollback**.

### 2.1.3 Middleware — Deep Dive

Middleware abstracts the *complexity* of the underlying heterogeneous IoT environment. The KTU curriculum emphasizes the following middleware service categories:

1. **Device Management Middleware** — registration, configuration, firmware updates (e.g., **LWM2M, OMA-DM**).
2. **Data Acquisition & Storage Middleware** — time-series databases (e.g., **InfluxDB, TimescaleDB**).
3. **Communication Middleware** — protocol translation (e.g., **MQTT broker, CoAP server, AMQP**).
4. **Security Middleware** — authentication, encryption (e.g., **TLS/DTLS handshakers**).
5. **Context-Awareness Middleware** — semantic reasoning over sensor data (e.g., **Fiware, IoT-EPI**).

### 2.1.4 Application Software — Deep Dive

IoT application software can reside in **three locations**:

- **On-device** (edge/fog) — for latency-critical tasks like motor control.
- **On-gateway** (edge computing) — running ML inference on a Jetson Nano.
- **On-cloud** (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT) — for big-data analytics and dashboards.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics for IoT Software Evaluation (frequently asked in KTU exams):**

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Typical IoT Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kernel ROM Footprint | $M_{ROM} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i$ where $S_i$ = size of each module | $10 - 100$ | KB |
| RAM Usage | $M_{RAM} = S_{stack} + S_{heap} + S_{data}$ | $4 - 64$ | KB |
