- **Nominal Range:** **10 m** (Class 2 — most common in IoT)
- **Max Raw Data Rate (v1.2):** 1 Mbps; (v2.0 + EDR): 3 Mbps
- **Max Active Devices in a Piconet:** **7 slaves + 1 master = 8**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bluetooth Protocol Stack — Layer-by-Layer Logic

Bluetooth is not a single protocol; it is a **vertical stack of cooperating layers** that map directly onto the OSI model. KTU examiners love to ask "Explain the Bluetooth protocol stack with a neat diagram" for 7–14 marks. Master the *why* behind every layer.

### 2.1.1 Radio (Physical) Layer
- **Why it exists:** To transmit and receive over the 2.4 GHz ISM band.
- **How it works:** Uses Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK) for 1 Mbps; π/4-DQPSK and 8-DPSK for EDR.
- **Engineering utility:** The hop synthesizer and antenna design live here.

### 2.1.2 Baseband Layer
- **Why it exists:** To define the *physical channel*, *frame format*, and *timing*.
- **How it works:** Manages **625 µs time slots**, master transmits on even slots, slaves on odd slots (TDD scheme).
- **Key concept:** Defines **Access Codes**, **Packet Headers**, and **Payload**.

### 2.1.3 Link Manager Protocol (LMP)
- **Why it exists:** To set up, authenticate, and tear down **logical links** between two Bluetooth devices.
- **How it works:** Handles pairing, encryption key generation, power-mode negotiation (Sniff, Hold, Park).
- **KTU buzzword:** *"LMP manages the link, not the data."*

### 2.1.4 Host Controller Interface (HCI)
- **Why it exists:** The **boundary** between the hardware radio module and the host software stack.
- **How it works:** Standardized commands over UART, USB, or SDIO.

### 2.1.5 Logical Link Control & Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)
- **Why it exists:** To multiplex multiple higher-layer protocols over a single ACL link; performs segmentation and reassembly.
- **How it works:** Provides **connection-oriented (CO)** and **connectionless (CL)** channels; supports QoS.

### 2.1.6 Service Discovery Protocol (SDP)
- **Why it exists:** For a device to **discover what services** another device offers (e.g., "Is this a keyboard? a headset?").
- **How it works:** Uses a UUID-based query-response mechanism.

### 2.1.7 RFCOMM (Cable Replacement Protocol)
- **Why it exists:** To **emulate a serial RS-232 cable** over Bluetooth, enabling legacy serial-port applications to run wirelessly.
- **How it works:** A transport layer on top of L2CAP; supports up to 60 simultaneous emulated serial ports.

### 2.1.8 OBEX / Application Profiles
- **Why it exists:** Standardized end-user profiles (HID, A2DP, HFP, SPP, PAN, etc.) sit on top of RFCOMM or L2CAP.

## 2.2 Piconet — Operational Logic

A **Piconet** is the *fundamental ad-hoc topology* of Bluetooth. It is formed when one device (the **Master**) broadcasts its clock and device address, and up to **7** other devices **synchronize** their hopping sequence and clock to the master.

### Step-by-Step Piconet Formation
1. **Inquiry Phase** — Master broadcasts an **ID packet**; any device in INQUIRY_SCAN state responds with its address and clock.
2. **Paging Phase** — Master pages the desired slave using the slave's device access code (DAC).
3. **Slave Response** — Slave enters PAGE_SCAN, acknowledges, and both devices exchange a Frequency Hop Synchronization (FHS) packet.
4. **Connection State** — Slave is now *connected*, follows the master's hop sequence, and is assigned a 3-bit **AM_ADDR** (Active Member Address, 1–7).

## 2.3 Scatternet — Inter-Piconet Bridging

A **Scatternet** is formed when a device participates in **two or more piconets simultaneously**, sharing its time between them.

- A device that is master in one piconet **cannot be master** in another at the same instant.
- A device is most often a **slave in one piconet** and **master in another**, or **slave in many**.
- Maximum inter-piconet traffic is limited because the device must switch its hop sequence.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities below are **KTU-approved** (sourced from IEEE 802.15.1 and Bluetooth SIG Core Spec v1.2). Memorize units verbatim.

| Quantity | Symbol / Value | Formula or Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of RF channels | $N_{ch}$ | 79 (classic) / 40 (BLE) | channels |
| Channel bandwidth | $BW$ | **1 MHz** per channel | MHz |
| Hop rate | $f_h$ | 1600 | hops/s |
| Slot duration | $T_s$ | $\dfrac{1}{2 f_h} = \dfrac{1}{3200} = 312.5$ | µs |
| **Dwell time per slot** | $T_{dwell}$ | $\dfrac{1}{f_h} = 625$ | µs |
| Max active piconet members | $N_{pico}$ | $1 \text{ master} + 7 \text{ slaves}$ | nodes |
| Max AM_ADDR value | $AM_{max}$ | $2^3 - 1 = 7$ | — |
| Frequency range | $f_{ISM}$ | $2400 - 2483.5$ | MHz |
| Center frequency of channel $k$ | $f_c(k)$ | $2402 + k$ | MHz, $k \in \lbrace 0, 1, \ldots, 78 \rbrace$ |
| Hop sequence selection | — | $\sigma(k+1) = \bigl( \sigma(k) + a + b \cdot \sigma'(k) \bigr) \bmod 79$ | — |
| Max piconets in a scatternet | $N_{scat}$ | Up to 10 piconets interlinked | piconets |
| EDR max data rate | $R_{EDR}$ | 3 | Mbps |
| Classic (v1.2) data rate | $R_{classic}$ | 1 | Mbps |

> **Note on notation:** Absolute-value or modulus operations are written using `\lbrace \rbrace` and `\bmod` to keep the markdown table parser stable — the KTU exam script *also* prefers $\bmod$ over the typographic `|` bar.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bluetooth's piconet/scatternet model is the **conceptual ancestor** of modern mesh IoT (Zigbee, Thread, BLE Mesh). The 7-slave cap forces **frequent scatternet bridging** in dense IoT deployments (e.g., a smart home with 50 sensors), which is *why* the Bluetooth SIG evolved toward **Bluetooth 5.x Mesh** with managed flooding — directly addressing the limitations of classic piconet.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Hop Frequency of Channel $k$

The **center frequency** of the $k$-th RF channel in classic Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15.1) is given by:

$$
f_c(k) \;=\; 2402 \;+\; k \quad \text{MHz}, \qquad k \in \lbrace 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 78 \rbrace
$$

**Step-by-step transition (for the KTU 14-marker):**

**Step 1.** The ISM band spans from 2.400 GHz to 2.4835 GHz, giving a usable width of

$$
\Delta f_{ISM} \;=\; 2483.5 \;-\; 2400.0 \;=\; 83.5 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 2.** A 1 MHz guard band is reserved at the lower edge (2.400–2.402 GHz), so usable spectrum is

$$
\Delta f_{usable} \;=\; 83.5 \;-\; 1.0 \;+\; 0.5 \;=\; 78.5 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 3.** With channel bandwidth $BW = 1$ MHz, the number of channels is

$$
N_{ch} \;=\; \left\lfloor \dfrac{\Delta f_{usable}}{BW} \right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor \dfrac{78.5}{1.0} \right\rfloor \;=\; 78
$$

**Step 4.** The IEEE 802.15.1 standard specifies **79 channels** (not 78) to allow a 1 MHz guard at the upper edge, giving $f_c(0) = 2402$ MHz and $f_c(78) = 2480$ MHz.

> **Conclusion (model answer line):** *The first channel sits at 2402 MHz, and each subsequent channel is offset by 1 MHz, producing the last channel at 2480 MHz — total 79 channels.*

## 3.2 Derivation — Maximum Throughput in a Piconet

We want the **maximum theoretical symmetric data rate** a master can sustain with **7 active slaves** using Asynchronous Connection-Less (ACL) packets.

**Step 1.** Slot duration:

$$
T_s \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2 f_h} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot 1600} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{3200} \text{ s} \;=\; 312.5 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 2.** One full duplex cycle (master TX + slave TX) occupies **2 slots**:

$$
T_{cycle} \;=\; 2 \cdot T_s \;=\; 625 \text{ µs} \;=\; T_{dwell}
$$

**Step 3.** With 1 master + 7 slaves, a round-robin poll takes **7 × 2 = 14 slots**. The number of full cycles per second is

$$
N_{cycles} \;=\; \dfrac{f_h}{7} \;=\; \dfrac{1600}{7} \;\approx\; 228.57 \text{ cycles/s per slave}
$$

**Step 4.** A 5-slot DH5 packet (EDR, 3 Mbps) carries 1024 information bytes. Theoretical peak rate per slave:

$$
R_{slave} \;=\; N_{cycles} \cdot L_{DH5} \cdot 8 \;\approx\; 228.57 \cdot 1024 \cdot 8 \;\approx\; 1.872 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 5.** Piconet ceiling (best case, all slaves active, DH5):

$$
R_{pico} \;=\; 7 \cdot R_{slave} \;\approx\; 13.1 \text{ Mbps}
$$

> **KTU Caveat:** In practice, poll traffic, retransmissions, and scatternet switching drop this to **< 2.1 Mbps aggregate** — a popular "limitations" question.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Piconet vs. Scatternet vs. BLE Mesh

| Property | Piconet | Scatternet | BLE Mesh |
|---|---|---|---|
| Topology | Star (1 master + ≤7 slaves) | Inter-connected piconets | Managed-flood mesh |
| Max nodes in a single piconet | **8** | 8 × number of piconets | **32 767** |
| Hopping pattern | Synchronized FHSS | Multiple FHSS, time-shared | Advertising channels, no FHSS |
| Master required? | Yes | Yes (one per piconet) | No (any node can relay) |
| Synchronization | Master clock | Master clocks (one per pico) | Self-organizing |
| Typical KTU use case | Headset pairing | Phone + smartwatch + car | Smart lighting / sensor web |

## 3.4 Derivation — Why Exactly 7 Slaves?

The baseband header carries a 3-bit **LT_ADDR** (Logical Transport Address) field for active slaves. The master reserves `LT_ADDR = 0` for the broadcast address, leaving:

$$
N_{slaves} \;=\; 2^3 \;-\; 1 \;=\; 7
$$

> **Conclusion:** *The 7-slave cap is a direct consequence of the 3-bit LT_ADDR header field — increasing it would require a wider baseband header, which would lower payload efficiency.*

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Channel Time-Slot Mapping

**Q (KTU Style):** A master polls 5 slaves in round-robin using DH1 packets (1 slot, 30 bytes info). Find the total poll cycle time and the per-slave poll rate.

**Step 1.** Each DH1 exchange (master + slave) = 2 slots.

$$
T_{poll} \;=\; 5 \times 2 \times T_s \;=\; 5 \times 2 \times 312.5 \text{ µs} \;=\; 3125 \text{ µs} \;=\; 3.125 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2.** Per-slave poll rate:

$$
f_{poll} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_{poll}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{3.125 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 320 \text{ polls/s}
$$

**Step 3.** Per-slave useful throughput (DH1, 30 B):

$$
R_{useful} \;=\; f_{poll} \cdot 30 \cdot 8 \;=\; 320 \cdot 240 \;=\; 76.8 \text{ kbps}
$$

> **Model Answer Statement:** *The master spends 3.125 ms per poll cycle, achieving 320 polls/s per slave and 76.8 kbps useful rate — substantially below the 1 Mbps raw rate due to poll overhead.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bluetooth Protocol Stack — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app[Application Profiles HID A2DP HFP SPP PAN] --> obex[OBEX Object Exchange]
    app --> rfcomm[RFCOMM Cable Replacement Serial]
    rfcomm --> l2cap[L2CAP Logical Link Control and Adaptation]
    obex --> l2cap
    l2cap --> sdp[SDP Service Discovery Protocol]
    l2cap --> hci[HCI Host Controller Interface]
    hci --> lmp[LMP Link Manager Protocol]
    lmp --> baseband[Baseband Link Controller]
    baseband --> radio[Radio 2.4 GHz FHSS GFSK]
    sdp --> l2cap
```

> **Reading guide (for the KTU answer script):** The top three boxes (Application, OBEX, RFCOMM) are *host-side software layers*, HCI is the *boundary*, and LMP, Baseband, Radio are *controller-side firmware/hardware*.

## 4.2 Piconet vs. Scatternet — Topology Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph picoA["PICONET A"]
        mA[Master A]:::master
        s1A[Slave 1]:::slave
        s2A[Slave 2]:::slave
        s3A[Slave 3]:::slave
        mA --- s1A
        mA --- s2A
        mA --- s3A
    end
    subgraph picoB["PICONET B"]
        mB[Master B]:::master
        s1B[Slave 1]:::slave
        s2B[Slave 2]:::slave
        bridge[BRIDGE NODE]:::bridge
        mB --- s1B
        mB --- s2B
        mB --- bridge
    end
    s2A --- bridge
    classDef master fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef slave fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef bridge fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Legend:** Yellow = Master, Blue = Slave, Red = Bridge (slave in piconet A AND master in piconet B). This is the **canonical scatternet figure** KTU examiners expect for a 14-mark question.

## 4.3 Time-Slot Alternation — Master vs. Slave TDD

```mermaid
gantt
    title Bluetooth TDD Slot Allocation (625 us dwell)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S
    section Master TX
    M-TX slot 0   :a0, 0, 312us
    M-TX slot 2   :a1, 625us, 312us
    M-TX slot 4   :a2, 1250us, 312us
    section Slave TX
    S-TX slot 1   :b0, 312us, 312us
    S-TX slot 3   :b1, 937us, 312us
    S-TX slot 5   :b2, 1562us, 312us
```

> **Reading guide:** Master always transmits on **even-numbered slots** (0, 2, 4, …), slaves on **odd-numbered slots** (1, 3, 5, …). This Time-Division Duplex (TDD) discipline prevents collision within a piconet.

## 4.4 FHSS Hop Sequence — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clk[Master Clock 3.2 kHz]:::clk --> gen[Hop Selector Algorithm]:::proc
    addr[Master BD_ADDR 48 bit]:::addr --> gen
    key[Encryption Key optional]:::key --> gen
    gen --> synth[Frequency Synthesizer]:::proc
    synth --> ant[2.4 GHz Antenna]:::hw
    ant --> air((Air)):::air
    classDef clk fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef addr fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef key fill:#FFF5E0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#D0D0FF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef air fill:#000,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

> **Reading guide:** The hop sequence is a deterministic function of (master clock, master BD_ADDR, optional encryption key). All slaves that know these three inputs will land on the **same frequency at the same instant** — this is the synchronization magic of piconet FHSS.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: List the layers of the Bluetooth protocol stack. Why is HCI called a "boundary" layer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Bluetooth stack consists of: (i) **Radio**, (ii) **Baseband**, (iii) **Link Manager Protocol (LMP)**, (iv) **Host Controller Interface (HCI)**, (v) **Logical Link Control & Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)**, (vi) **Service Discovery Protocol (SDP)**, (vii) **RFCOMM**, and (viii) **Application/OBEX profiles** *(1 mark)*.
HCI is called a *boundary* layer because it **separates the host-side software stack (L2CAP and above) from the controller-side hardware (LMP, Baseband, Radio)** *(1 mark)*. Commands cross this boundary over standardized transports (UART, USB, SDIO) *(1 mark)*.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is a Piconet? Why is the maximum number of active slaves limited to 7?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Piconet** is an ad-hoc network of **1 master + up to 7 active slaves** synchronized to the master's clock and frequency-hop pattern *(1 mark)*.
The 7-slave limit arises because the baseband packet header contains a **3-bit LT_ADDR (Logical Transport Address) field**; $2^3 - 1 = 7$ is the maximum slave count *(1 mark)*. Any more slaves would require a wider header, reducing payload efficiency *(1 mark)*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. *Writing "Piconet supports unlimited devices" or "master can have 7 slaves only in scatternet" — costs 2 marks.*
> 2. *Confusing LT_ADDR with AM_ADDR (3-bit) and PM_ADDR (8-bit, parked). Many students write AM_ADDR = 7; the limit comes from LT_ADDR.*
> 3. *Forgetting to mention FHSS in piconet — examiners deduct 1 mark per missing keyword.*
> 4. *Drawing a Piconet as a *bus* or *ring* — it is a *star*. One mark deducted.*
> 5. *In limitations, omitting "interference with Wi-Fi" — 2-mark question, very common.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Draw and explain the Bluetooth protocol stack. Compare it briefly with the OSI model.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Stack Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the seven layers in order from bottom: Radio → Baseband → LMP → HCI → L2CAP → SDP/RFCOMM/OBEX → Application Profiles. Mark the HCI boundary clearly.

**Step 2 — Layer Functions (3 marks):**
- **Radio:** 2.4 GHz ISM, GFSK modulation, 79 channels, 1 MHz spacing.
- **Baseband:** Defines physical channel, TDD slots (625 µs), access codes, packet formats (DH1, DM1, …).
- **LMP:** Link setup, authentication, encryption, power modes (Sniff, Hold, Park).
- **HCI:** Command/event interface between host and controller.
- **L2CAP:** Multiplexing, segmentation, QoS.
- **SDP:** Service discovery via UUIDs.
- **RFCOMM:** RS-232 cable emulation.
- **Application:** Profiles (HSP, HFP, A2DP, HID, SPP, etc.)

**Step 3 — OSI Comparison (1 mark):** Radio+Baseband ≈ Physical + Data Link; LMP ≈ Data Link management; L2CAP ≈ Network/Transport; SDP/RFCOMM/Apps ≈ Session/Presentation/Application.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: With a neat diagram, explain Piconet and Scatternet. State the address-limit reason.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Piconet Definition (2 marks):** A Piconet is a star topology of **1 master + ≤7 active slaves** synchronized via master's clock and 79-channel FHSS (1600 hops/s). All slaves share the master's BD_ADDR-derived hop sequence.

**Step 2 — Piconet Diagram (1 mark):** Master at center, 7 slaves around.

**Step 3 — Scatternet Definition (2 marks):** A Scatternet consists of **multiple piconets sharing one or more bridge devices**. A bridge is slave in one piconet and master in another (or slave in many). Up to 10 piconets can be interlinked.

**Step 4 — Address-Limit Reason (1 mark):** 3-bit LT_ADDR ⇒ max 7 active slaves.

**Step 5 — Practical Implications (1 mark):** Scatternet switching is time-shared → throughput drops; hence modern IoT prefers BLE Mesh.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the Bluetooth frequency hopping scheme. Derive the center frequency of the 40th channel and the slot duration.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — FHSS Concept (2 marks):** Bluetooth hops across 79 RF channels (2.402–2.480 GHz) at 1600 hops/s, dwelling 625 µs per channel. The hop pattern is a function of the master's clock and BD_ADDR, ensuring only synchronized slaves follow.

**Step 2 — Slot Duration Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
T_s = \dfrac{1}{2 f_h} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 1600} = 312.5 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 3 — Center Frequency of 40th Channel (2 marks):**

$$
f_c(40) = 2402 + 40 = 2442 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Insight (1 mark):** With 79 channels, the probability of collision with a Wi-Fi interferer (which uses 13 channels of 22 MHz) is minimized by the rapid hop; only ~22/79 ≈ 28 % of hops see overlap.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: List and briefly explain at least 6 limitations of Bluetooth technology in IoT deployments.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Limited Range (1 m – 100 m depending on class):** Class 2 is only 10 m, insufficient for many smart-home/industrial IoT scenarios *(1 mark)*.
2. **Piconet Size Cap (8 nodes):** Restricts star-topology scalability; large deployments need scatternet, which adds complexity *(1 mark)*.
3. **Interference with Wi-Fi:** Both share 2.4 GHz ISM; Wi-Fi channels overlap ~22 MHz wide, causing throughput drop in dense Wi-Fi environments *(1 mark)*.
4. **Low Data Rate (1–3 Mbps classic; 2 Mbps BLE):** Unsuitable for video/audio streaming beyond A2DP; BLE is even lower (~2 Mbps) *(1 mark)*.
5. **Power Consumption (Classic):** Although BLE mitigates this, classic Bluetooth's inquiry/paging overhead drains batteries in IoT sensor nodes *(1 mark)*.
6. **Scatternet Throughput Penalty:** Bridge nodes must time-share, and re-synchronization to a new piconet's FHSS pattern is non-trivial, halving throughput in practice *(1 mark)*.
7. **Security Concerns:** PIN-based legacy pairing (v2.0 and below) is vulnerable to brute-force; only SSP (Secure Simple Pairing) in v2.1+ is robust *(1 mark)*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Bluetooth Technology (Module 3)**

- [x] **Bluetooth = IEEE 802.15.1** WPAN, **2.4 GHz ISM**, GFSK modulation.
- [x] **79 channels × 1 MHz**, spanning **2402–2480 MHz**.
- [x] **Hop rate = 1600 hops/s**, dwell time **625 µs**, slot duration **312.5 µs**.
- [x] **Piconet = 1 master + ≤ 7 slaves** (3-bit LT_ADDR is the reason).
- [x] **Scatternet = interlinked piconets** via bridge devices (slave–master or slave–slave bridge).
- [x] **Master transmits on even slots, slaves on odd slots** (TDD).
- [x] **Bluetooth Stack layers (bottom → top):** Radio → Baseband → LMP → **HCI (boundary)** → L2CAP → SDP / RFCOMM / OBEX → Application Profiles.
- [x] **L2CAP** multiplexes higher layers; **RFCOMM** emulates RS-232; **SDP** discovers services.
- [x] **Piconet formation** = Inquiry → Page → Slave Response → Connected (AM_ADDR assigned).
- [x] **Limitations to memorize:** range, piconet cap, Wi-Fi interference, low data rate, scatternet overhead, classic power drain, legacy security.
- [x] **Maximum piconet members** = 7 slaves + 1 master = 8 (use 2³ − 1 logic in derivations).
- [x] **f_c(k) = 2402 + k MHz**, valid for $k \in \lbrace 0, 1, \ldots, 78 \rbrace$.
- [x] **KTU exam buzz-words:** *FHSS, TDD, AM_ADDR, LT_ADDR, piconet, scatternet, HCI boundary, L2CAP, RFCOMM, SDP, GFSK, EDR, BLE*.
- [x] **Always draw** the HCI split in protocol-stack answers — examiners mark 1 mark for that boundary line.
- [x] **Don't confuse** piconet (1 hop pattern) with scatternet (multiple hop patterns, time-shared).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bluetooth Low Energy - Key Features, Architecture, Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET634 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bluetooth Low Energy - Key Features, Architecture, Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)**, also marketed as **Bluetooth Smart**, is a wireless personal area network (WPAN) technology introduced as part of the **Bluetooth Core Specification 4.0** (released in 2010) and significantly enhanced in subsequent versions (4.1, 4.2, 5.0, 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 5.4). BLE is engineered from the ground up as a low-power, short-range communication protocol optimized for **Internet of Things (IoT)** devices, wearable electronics, healthcare sensors, and battery-operated edge nodes.

Unlike **Classic Bluetooth (BR/EDR)**, which is optimized for continuous audio/video streaming, BLE is designed for **periodic, low-duty-cycle transmission** of small data packets. The protocol operates in the globally available unlicensed **2.4 GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) band** (2.400 GHz – 2.4835 GHz) and employs **Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK)** modulation with a channel spacing of **2 MHz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** BLE is one of the most examination-relevant short-range wireless protocols. Students must memorize its **channel structure (40 channels: 3 advertising + 37 data)**, **state machine**, and **protocol stack** for Part B (14-mark) questions.

### Bluetooth Specification Evolution Table

| Specification | Year Released | Key BLE Advancement |
|---------------|---------------|---------------------|
| Bluetooth 4.0 (BLE) | 2010 | Introduction of BLE |
| Bluetooth 4.1 | 2013 | Coexistence improvement, IoT ready |
| Bluetooth 4.2 | 2014 | LE Data Packet Length Extension, IPv6 over BLE |
| Bluetooth 5.0 | 2016 | 2× speed, 4× range, 8× broadcasting capacity |
| Bluetooth 5.1 | 2019 | Direction Finding (AoA / AoD) |
| Bluetooth 5.2 | 2020 | LE Audio (LC3 codec), LE Isochronous Channels |
| Bluetooth 5.3 | 2021 | Connection subrating, channel classification |
| Bluetooth 5.4 | 2023 | PAwR (Periodic Advertising with Responses), EAD |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lighthouse and the Boat" Model

Imagine a vast, foggy ocean (the 2.4 GHz RF spectrum) where ships (IoT sensors) periodically need to send a brief signal ("My temperature is 25°C") to a coastal lighthouse (a smartphone or gateway).

- The **lighthouse (Advertiser)** periodically rotates and sends out a powerful, short **light flash (Advertising Packet)** on three special "advertising channels" that cut through the fog efficiently.
- A small **boat (Scanner)** listens on these advertising channels. When it sees the flash, it **wakes up briefly** to establish a handshake and receive the data.
- After the brief exchange, the boat goes back to **deep sleep** to conserve fuel (battery). The lighthouse also dims.

This is precisely how BLE operates: instead of maintaining a continuous high-bandwidth connection, devices **wake up, exchange a tiny data packet, and sleep** — a paradigm that allows a coin-cell battery to power a BLE sensor for **months or even years**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 40 channels and not one?** Classic Bluetooth hops across **79 channels** of 1 MHz each. BLE uses only **40 channels** of 2 MHz each. Fewer channels mean faster frequency synthesis, less time spent scanning, and dramatically reduced power consumption.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of BLE Design Philosophy

1. **Ultra-Low Power:** Peak current consumption in BLE 5.0 is as low as **~7.5 mA (TX at 0 dBm)** and **~7.5 mA (RX)**, with sleep currents in the **microampere range (typically <1 µA)**. A standard CR2032 coin cell (~220 mAh) can sustain a BLE beacon for **1–2 years**.
2. **Low Cost:** BLE SoCs (System-on-Chip) are available for **< $1 USD** in volume, with integrated radios, microcontrollers, and stacks.
3. **Ease of Deployment:** Devices can be discovered and paired in **< 3 ms** (BLE 5.0), making the user experience frictionless.

---

### Key Standardized Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Frequency Band | **2.4 GHz ISM (2.400 – 2.4835 GHz)** |
| Number of RF Channels | **40 (3 advertising + 37 data)** |
| Channel Bandwidth | **2 MHz** |
| Modulation | **GFSK (Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying)** |
| PHY Data Rate (LE 1M) | **1 Mbps** |
| PHY Data Rate (LE 2M) | **2 Mbps** (BLE 5.0+) |
| PHY Data Rate (LE Coded) | **125 kbps / 500 kbps** (BLE 5.0+) |
| Maximum TX Power | **+20 dBm (100 mW)** — typically +0 to +10 dBm |
| Nominal Range | **~10 m (indoor) to ~100 m (outdoor)** |
| Encryption | **AES-128 CCM** |
| Connection Setup Time | **3 ms (minimum)** |
| Maximum Payload (DLE) | **244 bytes** (with Data Length Extension) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Twelve Key Features of Bluetooth Low Energy

BLE's design is governed by a set of well-defined features that distinguish it from Classic Bluetooth and other short-range protocols. These are **high-yield for KTU Part A (3-mark)** questions.

### Feature 1: 2.4 GHz ISM Band Operation
BLE operates in the globally unlicensed 2.400–2.4835 GHz band, the same band used by Wi-Fi, Zigbee, and microwaves. This band is divided into **40 RF channels** with a **2 MHz spacing**, indexed from **0 to 39**.

- **Channels 37, 38, 39** → **Advertising Channels** (used for device discovery, broadcasting, and connection initiation)
- **Channels 0–36** → **Data Channels** (used for connected communication after a link is established)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why only 3 advertising channels?** The three advertising channels (37, 38, 39) are intentionally placed at the **edges** of the 2.4 GHz band (2402, 2426, 2480 MHz) to **avoid the most congested Wi-Fi channels (1, 6, 11)** which sit in the middle of the band. This dramatically increases the probability of successful discovery in a noisy RF environment.

### Feature 2: Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK) Modulation
BLE uses **GFSK with a modulation index h = 0.5** and a **bandwidth-bit period product (BT) of 0.5**. A binary '1' is represented by a **positive frequency deviation** and '0' by a **negative frequency deviation**.

The **minimum frequency deviation** for a logical '1' is:
$$f_{dev} \geq 185 \text{ kHz}$$

### Feature 3: Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH)
Although BLE 4.0 used fixed channel selection, **BLE 4.1+ supports Adaptive Frequency Hopping**. The Link Layer maintains a **Channel Map** of 5 bytes (40 bits, one per channel). Channels marked 'used' are avoided during hopping, allowing coexistence with Wi-Fi and other interferers.

### Feature 4: Master / Slave Architecture with Star Topology
A BLE piconet consists of:
- **One Master** (typically a smartphone or gateway)
- **One or more Slaves** (sensors/actuators)

The master controls all timing (connection events, channel hopping sequence). A single master can theoretically maintain up to **2^31 slaves** in standby, but practical limits (memory, scheduling) reduce this to a few dozen active connections.

### Feature 5: Connection State with Connection Events
A connection is parameterized by:
- **Connection Interval (CI):** 7.5 ms to 4 s
- **Slave Latency (SL):** 0 to 499 (number of connection events the slave can skip)
- **Supervision Timeout (STO):** 100 ms to 32 s

$$T_{duty\_cycle} = \frac{CI \times (SL + 1)}{CI \times (SL + 1) + \text{sleep time}}$$

### Feature 6: Non-Connection Mode — Advertising and Scanning
Devices can transmit data **without forming a connection** by using **Advertising Packets**. This is the basis of **BLE Beacons** (iBeacon, Eddystone). The advertiser wakes, broadcasts, and sleeps with a configurable **Advertising Interval** (20 ms to 10.24 s).

### Feature 7: AES-128 Encryption
BLE provides **AES-128-bit encryption** in CCM mode (Counter with CBC-MAC) for confidentiality and integrity. The encryption keys are derived through a process involving:
1. **Temporary Key (TK)** — derived from a PIN or Just Works pairing
2. **Short Term Key (STK)** — used during legacy pairing
3. **Long Term Key (LTK)** — used for encrypted connections
4. **Identity Resolving Key (IRK)** — used for private address resolution
5. **Connection Signature Resolving Key (CSRK)** — used for signed data

### Feature 8: Generic Attribute Profile (GATT)
GATT defines how data is structured in a **Service → Characteristic → Descriptor** hierarchy. All data exchange in a BLE connection occurs through GATT operations (Read, Write, Notify, Indicate).

### Feature 9: Generic Access Profile (GAP)
GAP defines the **roles** (Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central), **modes** (Discoverable, Connectable, Non-connectable), and **procedures** (Advertising, Scanning, Connection Establishment, Bonding).

### Feature 10: Low Power — Multiple Power Modes
- **Active Mode:** Full radio operation (~5–15 mA)
- **Sleep Mode:** CPU off, RAM retained (~1–5 µA)
- **Deep Sleep:** Everything off, wake on interrupt (<1 µA)

### Feature 11: Multi-Vendor Interoperability
The Bluetooth SIG (Special Interest Group) certifies all BLE products, guaranteeing interoperability across manufacturers.

### Feature 12: Short-Range, Low-Cost Hardware
A typical BLE SoC (e.g., Nordic nRF52832, TI CC2640) integrates:
- A 2.4 GHz radio
- A 32-bit ARM Cortex-M microcontroller
- 256–512 KB Flash
- 32–64 KB RAM
- Various peripherals (ADC, PWM, I2C, SPI)

All in a package as small as **3 mm × 3 mm**, costing **< $2** in volume.

## 2.2 The BLE Protocol Stack (Architecture)

The BLE stack is **layered**, following a hybrid Controller-Host architecture (similar to a simplified OSI model). For KTU, you must know **every layer and its function**.

### Complete BLE Protocol Stack

| Layer | Name | Function | Lives In |
|-------|------|----------|----------|
| **Application Layer** | Apps, Profiles | User logic, GATT clients/servers | Host MCU |
| **Framework** | BLE Stack Library (e.g., Zephyr, NimBLE) | API abstraction | Host MCU |
| **GATT** | Generic Attribute Profile | Service/Characteristic data exchange | Host |
| **ATT** | Attribute Protocol | Read/Write/Notify operations on attributes | Host |
| **L2CAP** | Logical Link Control & Adaptation | Protocol multiplexing, segmentation/reassembly | Host |
| **SMP** | Security Manager Protocol | Pairing, bonding, encryption | Host |
| **GAP** | Generic Access Profile | Roles, modes, discovery, connections | Host |
| **HCI** | Host Controller Interface | Command/event transport (UART/USB/SPI) | Interface |
| **Link Layer (LL)** | Link Layer | State machine, packet framing, channel hopping | Controller |
| **PHY** | Physical Layer | RF modulation, demodulation, TX/RX | Controller |

> [!NOTE]
> **HCI Split:** In a single-chip solution (typical for IoT), the HCI is internal and transparent. In a dual-chip solution (e.g., a BLE module + external MCU), HCI is exposed over UART, SPI, or USB.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation, boundary condition, and metric you will need for KTU numericals and derivations on this topic. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the pipe symbol to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Concept | Equation / Boundary Condition | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | RF Channels | $N_{ch} = 40 = N_{adv} + N_{data} = 3 + 37$ | channels | Index 0–39 |
| 2 | Channel Spacing | $\Delta f = 2$ | MHz | Per channel |
| 3 | Advertising Channel Frequencies | $f_k = 2402 + 2k$ for $k \in \{37,38,39\}$ | MHz | i.e., 2402, 2426, 2480 |
| 4 | Data Channel Frequencies | $f_k = 2402 + 2k$ for $k \in \{0,...,36\}$ | MHz | Index 0–36 |
| 5 | BLE 1M PHY Bit Rate | $R_b = 1$ | Mbps | Mandatory |
| 6 | BLE 2M PHY Bit Rate | $R_b = 2$ | Mbps | BLE 5.0+ |
| 7 | Symbol Rate (1M PHY) | $R_s = 1$ | Msps | 1 bit/symbol |
| 8 | GFSK Modulation Index | $h = 0.5$ | dimensionless | Bluetooth standard |
| 9 | Min Frequency Deviation | $f_{dev} \geq 185$ | kHz | For a logical '1' |
| 10 | Max TX Power (BLE 5.0) | $P_{TX,max} = +20$ | dBm | 100 mW |
| 11 | Connection Interval Range | $CI \in [7.5, 4000]$ | ms | Multiples of 1.25 ms |
| 12 | Slave Latency Range | $SL \in [0, 499]$ | events | Number of skipped events |
| 13 | Supervision Timeout Range | $STO \in [100, 32000]$ | ms | Link-loss detection |
| 14 | Advertising Interval Range | $T_{adv} \in [20, 10240]$ | ms | Multiples of 0.625 ms |
| 15 | Max Active Connections (master) | $N_{conn} \leq 2^{31}$ | slaves | Theoretical max |
| 16 | Encryption Block Size | $L_{AES} = 128$ | bits | AES-128 CCM |
| 17 | Legacy Max Payload | $L_{payload} = 31$ | bytes | Pre-DLE |
| 18 | DLE Max Payload | $L_{payload} = 244$ | bytes | BLE 4.2+ |
| 19 | Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | dB | For range estimation |
| 20 | Shannon-Hartley (BLE) | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps | Theoretic capacity |
| 21 | Power Budget (Link Budget) | $P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{path} + G_{RX}$ | dBm | For range calc |
| 22 | Range Estimation (open air) | $d \approx 10^{\frac{P_{TX} - P_{RX,min} - 32.45 - 20\log_{10}(f)}{20}}$ | meters | Outdoor approximation |
| 23 | MTU Size | $MTU \in [23, 517]$ | bytes | Negotiated by GATT |
| 24 | Connection Event Time | $T_{event} \geq 2 \cdot T_{IF} + N_{slots} \cdot 625 \mu s$ | ms | Where $T_{IF} = 150 \mu s$ |
| 25 | Sleep Current (typical) | $I_{sleep} < 1$ | µA | CR2032 friendly |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

BLE is not just an academic curiosity. It is the **workhorse of the consumer IoT revolution**:

- **Apple AirTags** — use BLE 5.0 + Ultra-Wideband (UWB) for finding lost items
- **Fitbit / Apple Watch / Garmin** — BLE for syncing health data to smartphones
- **Smart Locks (August, Yale)** — BLE for proximity-based unlocking
- **HVAC & Smart Buildings** — BLE Mesh for lighting and climate control
- **Medical Devices (Glucometers, Pulse Oximeters)** — BLE for patient monitoring (FDA-cleared)
- **Automotive (Digital Key 3.0)** — BLE for passive entry / passive start (PEPS)
- **Retail Beacons** — iBeacon (Apple) and Eddystone (Google) for in-store navigation
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — BLE sensors for predictive maintenance on rotating machinery

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-time tip for KTU:** If a question asks "List any 5 real-world applications of BLE" — the **healthcare wearables** and **smart home** categories are the most frequently accepted by KTU examiners.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: BLE Channel Frequency Calculation

The BLE specification defines a channel's center frequency as a linear function of the channel index $k$:

$$f_k = 2402 + 2k \text{ MHz}$$

We must show that this formula correctly yields the three advertising channels (37, 38, 39) at 2402, 2426, and 2480 MHz.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Establish the base frequency.**

The Bluetooth Core Specification defines the **starting frequency** of the BLE band as 2402 MHz, corresponding to the lower guard band. This is the center of channel index $k = 0$.

$$f_0 = 2402 + 2(0) = 2402 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 2 MHz spacing.**

The 2 MHz channel spacing is mandated by the regulatory requirements in the 2.4 GHz ISM band and is also chosen to be wide enough to support 1 Msps GFSK signals with sufficient roll-off.

$$f_1 = 2402 + 2(1) = 2404 \text{ MHz}$$
$$f_2 = 2402 + 2(2) = 2406 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the advertising channel frequencies.**

For channel 37:
$$f_{37} = 2402 + 2(37) = 2402 + 74 = 2476 \text{ MHz}$$

Wait — let us re-verify using the official Bluetooth specification. The official formulas are split:

For advertising channels ($k = 37, 38, 39$):
$$f_{adv} = 2402 + 2k$$

$$f_{37} = 2402 + 2(37) = 2476 \text{ MHz}$$
$$f_{38} = 2402 + 2(38) = 2478 \text{ MHz}$$
$$f_{39} = 2402 + 2(39) = 2480 \text{ MHz}$$

For data channels ($k = 0, 1, ..., 36$):
$$f_{data} = 2404 + 2k$$

$$f_0 = 2404 + 2(0) = 2404 \text{ MHz}$$
$$f_{12} = 2404 + 2(12) = 2428 \text{ MHz}$$
$$f_{36} = 2404 + 2(36) = 2476 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the gap structure.**

The lowest data channel (2404 MHz) is **2 MHz above** the lowest advertising channel (2402 MHz), and the highest data channel (2476 MHz) is **4 MHz below** the highest advertising channel (2480 MHz). This creates a **protective guard band** against interference from Wi-Fi channel 1 (centered at 2412 MHz) and other out-of-band emissions.

**Final Result:**

| Channel | Type | Frequency (MHz) |
|---------|------|-----------------|
| 37 | Advertising | 2402 |
| 0 | Data | 2404 |
| 1 | Data | 2406 |
| ... | Data | ... |
| 12 | Data | 2428 |
| 38 | Advertising | 2426 |
| ... | Data | ... |
| 36 | Data | 2476 |
| 39 | Advertising | 2480 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrected formula (KTU-correct version):**
> - Advertising channels: $f_k = 2402 + 2k$ for $k \in \{37, 38, 39\}$
> - Data channels: $f_k = 2404 + 2k$ for $k \in \{0, 1, ..., 36\}$
>
> The 2 MHz "step" before data channel 0 creates a deliberate guard band to minimize co-channel interference from Wi-Fi.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: BLE Range Estimation Using Free-Space Path Loss

Given a BLE transmitter power of $P_{TX} = +8$ dBm at 2.45 GHz, with isotropic antennas ($G_{TX} = G_{RX} = 0$ dBi) and a receiver sensitivity of $P_{RX,min} = -90$ dBm, derive the maximum outdoor range.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the Friis Transmission Equation in dB form.**

The received power in free space is:

$$P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{path} + G_{RX} \text{ (dB)}$$

where the free-space path loss (FSPL) is:

$$FSPL(d) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known frequency.**

$$f = 2450 \text{ MHz}$$

$$20\log_{10}(2450) = 20 \cdot 3.3892 = 67.78 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the maximum allowable path loss.**

For a successful link:
$$P_{RX} \geq P_{RX,min}$$

$$L_{path,max} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - P_{RX,min}$$

$$L_{path,max} = 8 + 0 + 0 - (-90) = 98 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for distance.**

$$98 = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 67.78$$

$$20\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 98 - 32.45 - 67.78 = -2.23 \text{ dB}$$

$$\log_{10}(d_{km}) = \frac{-2.23}{20} = -0.1115$$

$$d_{km} = 10^{-0.1115} \approx 0.774 \text{ km}$$

$$d \approx 774 \text{ meters}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{d_{max} \approx 774 \text{ m (outdoor, line-of-sight, free-space)}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply practical derating.**

Indoor / non-line-of-sight conditions typically reduce this by 10–20 dB, yielding a practical range of:

$$d_{practical} \approx 30 \text{–} 100 \text{ m (indoor)}$$

This matches the typical **"BLE range = 10 m indoor, 100 m outdoor"** claim found in vendor datasheets.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effective Throughput with Connection Parameters

A BLE peripheral uses a connection interval $CI = 100$ ms, slave latency $SL = 4$, and transmits 20-byte GATT notifications per connection event. What is the **effective downlink throughput**?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the actual connection event period.**

$$T_{event} = CI \times (SL + 1) = 100 \times (4 + 1) = 500 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of events per second.**

$$N_{events/s} = \frac{1000 \text{ ms/s}}{500 \text{ ms/event}} = 2 \text{ events/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bytes transmitted per second.**

$$R_{bytes} = N_{events/s} \times L_{payload} = 2 \times 20 = 40 \text{ bytes/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bits per second.**

$$R_{bits} = 40 \times 8 = 320 \text{ bps}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{R_{eff} = 320 \text{ bps}}$$

This is **3000× lower** than the 1 Mbps PHY rate — illustrating BLE's fundamental design: it is **not for throughput**, it is for **energy efficiency**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Battery Life Estimation

A BLE sensor uses a CR2032 coin cell (220 mAh, 3.0 V). Average current draw: 15 µA in sleep, 8 mA during TX (1 ms events). Connection interval = 1 s, slave latency = 0. Estimate battery life.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute current consumption per event cycle.**

In one cycle of 1 second:
- Sleep time: ~999 ms at 15 µA
- Active time: 1 ms at 8 mA = 0.001 s × 8 mA

**Step 2 — Compute average current.**

$$I_{avg} = I_{sleep} \times \frac{T_{sleep}}{T_{cycle}} + I_{active} \times \frac{T_{active}}{T_{cycle}}$$

$$I_{avg} = 15 \mu A \times \frac{999}{1000} + 8 mA \times \frac{1}{1000}$$

$$I_{avg} = 14.985 \mu A + 8 \mu A = 22.985 \mu A \approx 23 \mu A$$

**Step 3 — Compute battery life.**

$$t_{life} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{220 \text{ mAh}}{23 \mu A} = \frac{220{,}000 \mu A \cdot h}{23 \mu A} \approx 9565 \text{ h}$$

$$t_{life} \approx \frac{9565}{24} \approx 398 \text{ days} \approx 1.09 \text{ years}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\text{Battery Life} \approx 1 \text{ year (single CR2032)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Reality:** When self-discharge (typically 1–2% per year for CR2032) and additional overhead (CPU wake-up, sensor reads, encryption) are considered, the practical lifetime is **9–12 months** — exactly matching vendor marketing claims for BLE beacons.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Connection Parameter Trade-offs

| Parameter | Lower Value | Higher Value | Trade-off |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| Connection Interval (CI) | ↓ Latency, ↑ Power | ↑ Latency, ↓ Power | Throughput vs. Battery |
| Slave Latency (SL) | ↓ Latency, ↓ Power (synchronous) | ↑ Latency, ↓ Power (event skipping) | Responsiveness vs. Battery |
| Supervision Timeout (STO) | Fast link-loss detection | Robust to interference | Reliability vs. False disconnects |
| TX Power | ↓ Range, ↓ Power | ↑ Range, ↑ Power (linear) | Range vs. Battery |
| Advertising Interval | ↓ Discovery time, ↑ Power | ↑ Discovery time, ↓ Power | UX vs. Battery |
| MTU Size | ↓ Throughput per packet | ↑ Throughput per packet | Header overhead vs. Burst efficiency |
| PHY Rate (1M / 2M) | ↑ Range, ↓ Rate | ↓ Range, ↑ Rate (BLE 5.0) | Range vs. Speed |

## 4.2 BLE Link Layer State Machine

The Link Layer is the heart of BLE. Every BLE device transitions through these states. **This is a very high-yield KTU diagram** — it appears in nearly every past-year paper.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> Standby
    Standby --> Advertising : Enter Advertising Role
    Standby --> Scanning : Enter Scanning Role
    Standby --> Initiating : Enter Initiator Role

    Advertising --> Connection : Receive CONNECT_REQ
    Scanning --> Connection : Send CONNECT_REQ
    Initiating --> Connection : Receive ADV + Send CONNECT_REQ

    Connection --> Standby : Connection Terminated
    Connection --> Connection : Transmit Data Packets
    Advertising --> Standby : Stop Advertising
    Scanning --> Standby : Stop Scanning

    note right of Advertising : 3 advertising channels\nADV_IND, ADV_DIRECT_IND\nADV_NONCONN_IND, ADV_SCAN_IND
    note right of Scanning : Passive or Active\nScanning on 37 data channels\nplus 3 advertising channels
    note right of Connection : Connection Interval active\nChannel hopping on 37 data channels\nMaster controls timing
```
